French Cyber Arsenal
Training Pathway
EncroChat Joint Op
DGSE — Direction Générale de la Sécurité Extérieure
Paris | Est. 1982 | ~7,000 staff
France's foreign intelligence service — equivalent to CIA + MI6. Reports to Ministry of Defence. Alongside: DGSI (domestic, equivalent MI5), DRM (military), DRSD (defence counterintel).

Mission: Foreign intelligence, counterterrorism, counterproliferation, economic intelligence. Known for aggressive economic espionage against US/UK partners — "friendly" services that spy on allies (Boeing vs Airbus, Thompson vs Raytheon).

Technical Directorate (DT): DGSE's signals and cyber arm. Codenamed "La Piscine" (The Swimming Pool) — informal name from the pool next to Paris HQ. DT runs SIGINT collection, malware development, CNE operations.

Five Eyes Adjacent: France is NOT Five Eyes but has bilateral intel-sharing agreements with all members. SSCD (Signals/SIGINT sharing) with UK post-Brexit maintained. EU intel sharing via INTCEN.
► HQ: Boulevard Mortier, 20th arrondissement Paris — "La Piscine"
► Budget: ~€1.1B (2024 est.) — largest EU intelligence budget per capita
► DGSI: domestic counterpart, 4,200 staff, handles espionage cases in France
► Francafrique: DGSE maintains covert political networks across 20 African nations
► French law: Article L811-3 — broad SIGINT authority, no warrant for foreign targets
DGSEFranceIntelligenceLa PiscineSIGINT
ANSSI — Agence Nationale de la Sécurité des Systèmes d'Information
Est. 2009 | 600+ staff | Cyber Authority
France's national cybersecurity agency — GCHQ-NCSC / BSI equivalent. Attached to Prime Minister's office (not defence). Highest reputation in EU for technical quality of advisories.

Mandate: Protection of French CNI, government networks, and private sector. Mandatory incident reporting for OIV (Opérateurs d'Importance Vitale) — 200+ critical infrastructure operators in 12 sectors.

CERT-FR: ANSSI's CERT publishes detailed technical advisories (often better documented than US-CERT). Notable: SandWorm/Sandworm attribution 2020 (GRU), Lazarus advisories, APT31 (France-specific targeting 2021).

SecNumCloud: French government cloud security certification. Controversial: requires data sovereignty on French soil, effectively bans AWS GovCloud/Azure Government for French state data.

CLIP OS: ANSSI's own hardened Linux OS for government — open source, based on Gentoo. Mandatory compartmentalisation, TPM attestation, verified boot.
► OIV: 200+ critical operators, mandatory 24h incident notification to ANSSI
► APT31 advisory 2021: detailed attribution of Chinese hacking against French targets
► Sandworm/GRU 2020: ANSSI confirmed Sandworm attacked Centreon IT monitoring platform
► CLIP OS: open source at github.com/clipos — French hardened government OS
► ANSSI qualification: 3 tiers — CSPN (fast track), visa, certification (full eval)
ANSSIFranceCybersecurityCERT-FRSecNumCloud
COMCYBER — French Military Cyber Command
Ministère des Armées | Est. 2017
France's unified military cyber command. Created 2017 as peer to NSA/CYBERCOM (US), KSN (Germany), GCHQ (UK).

Organisation: COMCYBER commands: CALID (incident response/defence), CASSI (technical intelligence), C3P (cyber protection). ~3,500 uniformed cyber warriors, target 5,000 by 2025.

Offensive Mandate: France was first EU nation to officially acknowledge and legalise offensive cyber operations in the LPM (Military Programming Law) 2019. "Lutte Informatique Offensive" (LIO) — cyber attack operations formally authorised in armed conflict.

ARES: French military CTF and cyber exercise platform. Annual DEFNET exercise — largest French military cyber exercise, 300+ participants, tests joint response to simulated attacks on military networks.

Cyber Reserve: France created voluntary cyber reserve — civilian tech professionals can serve 30 days/year as cyber reservists. 4,000 target strength. Direct partnership with private sector CERT teams.
► LIO: legally authorised offensive operations — first in EU to formalise in law
► DEFNET 2024: simulated coordinated attack on military logistics/C2 systems
► Budget: €4B for cyber 2024-2030 (LPM 2024-2030)
► ESIGN: French military cybersecurity school, Saint-Cyr campus
► Cyber reservists: work at BNP Paribas, Thales, Airbus — then serve 30d/year
COMCYBERFranceMilitary CyberLIOOffensive
DGSE Notable Operations
Historical Intelligence Record
RAINBOW WARRIOR (1985): DGSE operatives sank Greenpeace ship in Auckland harbour to prevent interference with French nuclear tests in Pacific. Two operatives arrested, convicted by NZ courts. Diplomatic crisis with NZ/Netherlands. Highlighted DGSE willingness to operate against Western allies.

OPERATION SATANIQUE (1985): Same Rainbow Warrior op — operatives used false Swiss/French tourist identities, planted limpet mines. France initially denied then admitted and paid NZ $13M compensation.

ECONOMIC ESPIONAGE (1990s): DGSE targeted Boeing, Texas Instruments, IBM executives on Air France flights — bugs in seats, stewardesses recruited as informants. Gave intelligence to Airbus, Thomson. Exposed by CIA 1995.

MALI/SAHEL (2013-2022): Operation Serval then Barkhane — DGSE HUMINT and SIGINT supported French military strikes against jihadist groups. DGSE used signals intelligence for targeting strikes. ~5,000 French troops over 9 years.

ENCROCHAT (2020): Joint French/UK/Dutch operation cracked EncroChat criminal phone network (see dedicated panel).
► Rainbow Warrior: operatives posed as Swiss diving tourists "Alain and Sophie Turenge"
► Air France bugging: CIA tip to US companies in 1995 — full diplomatic incident
► Sahel SIGINT: DGSE ran drone and COMINT targeting for Barkhane strikes
► Françafrique: DGSE maintains paid informant network in 20 African head-of-state circles
DGSERainbow WarriorOperationsEconomic EspionageSahel
French Cyber Arsenal & SIGINT Infrastructure
DGSE Technical Directorate | CLASSIFIED
FRENCHELON: France's SIGINT network — separate from but parallel to ECHELON (Five Eyes). Intercepts satellite comms via stations in: Domme (Dordogne), New Caledonia, French Guiana, Djibouti, Réunion. Covers sub-Saharan Africa, Middle East, parts of Asia.

Cable Tapping: DGSE confirmed (leaked docs 2013) to tap undersea cables landing in France — Marseille is a major landing point for cables connecting Europe to Middle East/Africa/Asia. "La Piscine" analysis centre processes intercepts.

TEHTRIS: French cybersecurity firm with known DGSE/DRM alumni. Products used by French government. Representative of French sovereign cyber industrial base (alongside Thales, Atos, Orange Cyberdefense).

Malware Development: DGSE confirmed to develop offensive implants in-house (LPM 2019 acknowledgement). Known French-linked tools: "Animal Farm" APT cluster (Babar, Bunny, Casper, Nbot, Tafacalou) — attributed to DGSE by leaked CSE (Canadian SIGINT) slides.

Lawful Intercept: CNCTR oversight body approves warrants. Post-Snowden: "Loi Renseignement" 2015 gave DGSE/DGSI expanded metadata collection powers — "boîtes noires" (black boxes) installed at ISP level.
► Marseille cables: SMW3, SMW4, SEA-ME-WE 4 — connecting Europe to Asia/Middle East
► Animal Farm: French APT cluster attributed via leaked CSEC slide "SNOWGLOBE 2011"
► Babar malware: cartoon elephant icon, 2009-2012, targets: Middle East/Africa govts
► Loi Renseignement 2015: mass metadata collection legalised post-Charlie Hebdo attacks
► Budget DT (tech directorate): estimated 40% of total DGSE budget
DGSEFRENCHELONAnimal FarmCable TappingLawful Intercept
French Intelligence Training Pathway
DGSE / ANSSI / COMCYBER
Academic Route: Top grandes écoles feed DGSE/ANSSI: École Polytechnique (X), ENSTA, Télécom Paris, CentraleSupélec. INRIA (research institute) partnership with ANSSI for cryptography research.

EPITA & Epitech: Private engineering schools produce large proportion of French cybersecurity talent. EPITA's IG2I and IS2P tracks explicitly target government/defence careers.

FIC — Forum International de la Cybersécurité: Annual Lille conference, largest European cybersecurity event. ANSSI, COMCYBER, DGSE all recruit here. "Cyber Trophy" competition for students.

ESIGN: Ecole Supérieure et d'Application du Génie (military engineering school). Cyber specialisation track. Graduates commissioned directly into COMCYBER units.

Concours Orange: Orange Cyberdefense (French telco) runs national CTF — ANSSI/COMCYBER headhunters attend finals. Winner's CVs go directly to government security services.

DGSE Hiring: Advertises openly on dgse.gouv.fr for technical roles (TI/SIC). French citizenship mandatory. Extensive background check. Unusual: DGSE advertises data science/ML roles — AI for signal processing.
► Polytechnique X: produces 20% of ANSSI leadership; known DGSE alumni
► INRIA PROSECCO team: formal verification of cryptographic protocols for ANSSI
► FIC Lille: 15,000 attendees, government/industry/academic, annual in January
► DGSE job site: dgse.gouv.fr/fr/carriere — posts technical roles publicly
► Clearance time: French Top Secret (TSD) = 12-18 months investigation
DGSEFranceTrainingANSSIEPITA
Operation EncroChat — 2020
French/GCHQ/Dutch Joint Cyber Op | Criminal Network Dismantled
The largest coordinated law enforcement cyber operation in European history. Cracked EncroChat — a €1,000/device encrypted phone platform used by 60,000 criminals globally.

Background: EncroChat sold "hack-proof" Android phones with: dual OS (panic wipe), proximity sensors, encrypted VOIP/messaging, remote wipe, no GPS/camera/microphone. Servers in France and Netherlands.

French Method: DGSI (domestic, with DGSE technical support) obtained court order to access EncroChat server in Roubaix, France. Pushed a malicious OTA (Over-The-Air) software update to all 32,477 French-market devices. Update: harvested messages, contacts, photos BEFORE encryption. Ran March-June 2020.

Scale: 100M+ messages read in real-time. 60,000 users globally. UK share: 10,000 users, 27M messages. Europol coordinated with 11 countries. Results: 800 UK arrests (NCA), 6,500+ arrests EU-wide, €700M+ seized, 900+ weapons, 30+ tonnes drugs.

Legal Controversy: Defence lawyers challenged admissibility — was it mass surveillance? UK: Court of Appeal ruled EncroChat evidence admissible 2021. Germany: BGH ruled partially inadmissible. EU court challenge ongoing 2024.
► Malicious update: deployed via legitimate EncroChat update server to 32,477 devices
► Harvest: plaintext messages before E2E encryption applied — before keys generated
► UK NCA: 800 arrests, £54M cash, 900+ weapons, 1.8 tonnes cocaine from UK share alone
► Duration: March-June 2020 — 4 months of real-time criminal comms monitoring
► EncroChat device: €1,000 handset + €1,500/6-month subscription — criminal premium product
EncroChatFranceDGSIJoint OperationCriminal Intelligence
◆ CNI — CENTRO NACIONAL DE INTELIGENCIA
// SPAIN
CNI Overview
CCN-CERT Cyber
CatalanGate / Pegasus
Notable Operations
// TRADECRAFT
Spanish Cyber Arsenal
Training Pathway
EU Intelligence Sharing
CNI — Centro Nacional de Inteligencia
Madrid | Est. 2002 | ~3,000 staff
Spain's national intelligence service. Successor to CESID (Centro Superior de Información de la Defensa). Reports to President of Government via Secretary of State for Intelligence. Oversight: Congressional Intelligence Committee (classified sessions).

Mission: Foreign intelligence, counterintelligence, counterterrorism, proliferation threats, organised crime, cyber threats. Spain faces unique threats: Catalonian independence movement (domestic), Moroccan espionage (neighbour), jihadist networks (ETA replaced by ISIS recruitment).

Structure: CNI (foreign+cyber) + CGI (counter-intelligence) + CCN (national cryptology centre, runs CERT). All under one roof unlike UK (MI5/MI6 split) or US (CIA/FBI split).

EU Intelligence: Spain is active in EU INTCEN, Counter Terrorism Group (CTG), and Club de Berne (informal European intelligence exchange). Post-ETA period shifted focus to IS networks and Sahel threats.
► Legal basis: Ley 11/2002 de CNI — created modern service post-CESID scandal
► CESID scandal 1995: illegal wiretaps of politicians, journalists, King Juan Carlos I
► Budget: ~€400M (2024 estimate, largely classified)
► Director: reports directly to PM — confirmed by Parliament in closed session
► Club de Berne: informal EU-27 intelligence sharing, Spain founding member
CNISpainIntelligenceMadridEU
CCN-CERT — Centro Criptológico Nacional
National Cybersecurity Authority | Part of CNI
Spain's national CERT and cybersecurity authority. Unlike ANSSI (separate from intel) or NCSC (GCHQ subsidiary), CCN-CERT is directly inside CNI — tightest integration in EU.

Mandate: Protects Spanish government networks, CNI systems, critical infrastructure (Ministerio del Interior classification: 12 critical sectors). Mandatory 24h incident reporting for public sector and CNPIC (critical infrastructure operators).

CARMEN: CCN's own network monitoring platform. Analyses traffic at government network gateways. Blocks malicious connections in real-time. Distributed to ~400 public sector entities. Spanish sovereign alternative to commercial NDR products.

MICROCLAUDIA: CCN endpoint protection tool (2021). Free to Spanish public sector. Focused on ransomware prevention — specific response to 2021 SEPE (unemployment agency) Ryuk ransomware attack that paralysed services for 3 weeks.

CCN-STIC Guides: 900-series technical security configuration guides — equivalent of NIST SP 800 series. Cover: Windows hardening, Linux, network devices, cloud. Publicly available in Spanish.
► SEPE attack 2021: Ryuk ransomware, 710 offices offline, 500k unemployment claims delayed
► CARMEN: deployed at Red SARA (Spanish govt backbone) choke points
► CCN-STIC 617: main Windows 10 hardening guide — widely used in Spanish govt
► Hospital Clínic Barcelona 2023: RansomHouse attack, 4TB stolen, ER services disrupted
► CCN annual report: Spain experiences ~40,000 cyber incidents/year on public sector
CCN-CERTSpainCybersecurityCARMENMICROCLAUDIA
CatalanGate — Pegasus vs Catalan Independence Leaders
2017-2022 | Citizen Lab Investigation
The most significant domestic use of offensive spyware by an EU member state against its own citizens/officials. Exposed by Citizen Lab (Toronto) April 2022.

What Happened: At least 65 Catalan independence movement targets infected with Pegasus (NSO Group, Israeli), Candiru (another Israeli firm), and/or PREDATOR (Cytrox, North Macedonia). Targets included: President of Catalonia, MEPs, lawyers, activists, journalists covering independence movement.

Timeline: Earliest infections: 2017 (around independence referendum). Peak: 2019-2020. Citizen Lab forensically confirmed via iMazing analysis and network indicator matching. EU Parliament commissioned inquiry 2022.

Attribution: Citizen Lab assessed "high confidence" CNI responsible. Spanish government acknowledged some surveillance was authorised by courts (legal wiretaps). But Pegasus allows targeting beyond court-authorised scope — installing persistent implant, not just tapping specific communications.

Consequences: CNI Director Paz Esteban dismissed 2022 (rare accountability). EU Parliament vote: declared Pegasus use by member states incompatible with EU law (non-binding). NSO Group sued in multiple EU jurisdictions.
► 65 targets confirmed: presidents, ministers, MEPs, lawyers, journalists, civil society
► Technique: zero-click iMessage exploit (FORCEDENTRY era) — some targets clicked nothing
► CNI Director: Paz Esteban fired May 2022 — explicitly linked to CatalanGate
► EU PEGA committee: 8-month inquiry, recommended ban on Pegasus in EU, not binding
► NSO defence: all sales approved by Israeli government export licence
CatalanGateSpainPegasusCNICatalonia
CNI Notable Operations
Historical Spanish Intelligence
ETA (1959-2011): CNI/CESID primary domestic focus for 50+ years. ETA (Basque separatist terrorist group) targeted with: HUMINT penetration, SIGINT, joint ops with French DST/DGSI (cross-border). Declassified ops show: penetration of ETA command structure, double agents, signal interception of coded communications.

OPERATION CORONEL (2015): CNI coordinated with police to dismantle ISIS recruitment network in Ceuta/Melilla (Spanish territories in Morocco). 10 suspects arrested. First major IS disruption op by CNI using combined HUMINT + SIGINT.

MOROCCAN COUNTER-ESPIONAGE: Morocco (DGED intelligence) actively recruits in Ceuta, Melilla, and among Moroccan diaspora in Spain. CNI runs counter-intelligence operations against Moroccan intelligence activity. Ceuta/Melilla dispute creates permanent tension.

PATRIOT (2022): Spanish Defence Ministry (EMAD) confirmed CNI and defence systems were targeted by Pegasus — same spyware used against Catalans. Attribution: Moroccan intelligence (DGED). PM Sanchez and Defence Minister Margarita Robles phones infected. Diplomatic crisis with Rabat.

UKRAINE INTEL SHARING: Spain sharing CNI intelligence with Ukraine post-2022 invasion. CNI analysts embedded in EU INTCEN Ukraine task force.
► ETA: 829 killed over 50 years. CNI/CESID intelligence contributed to final ceasefire 2011
► Morocco Pegasus: PM Sanchez phone had 34 successful Pegasus extractions (Citizen Lab)
► Ceuta/Melilla: permanent CNI counter-intel focus against DGED (Moroccan intel)
► ISIS Spain: 230+ convicted of terrorism offences 2013-2023, CNI provided evidence in 140+ cases
CNISpainETAMoroccoISIS
Spanish Cyber Arsenal & Infrastructure
CNI / CCN / MCCD
MCCD — Mando Conjunto del Ciberespacio: Spain's military cyber command (created 2021, replacing previous EMACON cyber unit). 1,000+ military cyber operators. Offensive cyber mandate under Rules of Engagement authorised by Cabinet.

ISDEFE: State-owned defence engineering company. Key technical provider to CNI and MCCD. Develops: secure comms systems, cryptographic devices, network monitoring tools. Equivalent of UK's GCHQ Bude or US NIST role.

Indra Sistemas: Spanish defence/IT giant (state partial ownership). Provides: air traffic control, radar, electronic warfare, cybersecurity products to CNI/MCCD. Bidder on EU sovereign cloud/GAIA-X contracts.

National 5G Security Centre: CNI oversight of 5G rollout — Huawei effectively banned in critical infrastructure (not formal law but CNI recommendation applied). Orange Spain, Movistar, Vodafone España all received CNI risk assessments.

SIGINT Infrastructure: Spain operates signals collection from: Canary Islands station (Atlantic/Africa coverage), joint NATO SIGINT facilities. Participates in EU SIGINT sharing through INTCEN.
► MCCD: 1,000 operators, HQ Madrid, activated for "Hybrid Threats" response
► Indra: €4.2B revenue, 57,000 employees — Spain's Lockheed Martin
► Huawei: CNI classified assessment 2020 — details secret but exclusion from CNI networks confirmed
► Canary Islands: SIGINT position covers West Africa, Sahel, Atlantic shipping lanes
► STIC: CNI-developed classified comms network for Spanish govt (equivalent UK GSI)
SpainMCCDIndraSIGINTCyber Command
Spanish Cyber Training Pathway
CNI / CCN / MCCD Recruitment
University Pipeline: Top schools: Universidad Politécnica de Madrid (UPM), Universidad Complutense (UCM), Universitat Politècnica de Catalunya (UPC — Barcelona). UPM runs Máster en Ciberseguridad y Ciberdefensa in partnership with CCN.

CCN-CERT Training: CCN offers free cybersecurity courses via STIC portal (formacion.ccn-cert.cni.es). 500+ courses. From basic awareness to advanced RE, forensics, incident response. Open to public sector and some private sector.

CyberEx: National cybersecurity exercise organised by CCN annually. Tests public sector response to simulated multi-sector attack. 60+ organisations, 500+ participants. MCCD participates in military equivalent CIBER-X.

Military Route: Academia General Militar (Zaragoza) → specialisation in C2 systems → MCCD assignment. MCCD runs internal "Curso de Operaciones en el Ciberespacio" — classified curriculum, ~6 months.

CNI Recruitment: Competitive examination (oposiciones) — like French concours. Three categories: analyst, technical (IT/SIGINT), operational. Technical track: university degree + 9-month CNI training school. Background investigation: 18+ months.
► CCN training portal: 500+ courses free to public sector — formacion.ccn-cert.cni.es
► CyberEx 2023: simulated hybrid attack on energy grid + disinformation campaign simultaneously
► CNI oposicion: ~500 candidates per 20 places in technical track (25:1 ratio)
► ETSIT (Telecoms Engineering School UPM): highest CNI/CCN recruitment rate in Spain
► NATO CCDCOE: Spain joined 2016, officers rotate through Tallinn for training
SpainCNITrainingCCN-CERTMilitary
EU Intelligence Architecture — Spain's Role
INTCEN / CTG / EU SIGINT
EU INTCEN: EU Intelligence and Situation Centre — civilian intel analysis body. ~100 staff from member states. All 27 national services share assessments. Spain contributes CNI analysts, particularly on: North Africa, Sahel, Islamist extremism, Latin America (unique Spanish expertise).

Counter Terrorism Group (CTG): Informal body of EU+UK+Norway+Switzerland intelligence services. Shares real-time counterterrorism intelligence. Spain active since Madrid 11-M 2004 bombings (191 killed) — created urgency for EU intel sharing reform.

ENISA Partnership: European Union Agency for Cybersecurity based in Athens. Spain actively participates in: EU CyberCrisis Liaison Organisation (CyCLONe), Blueprint exercise for cross-border cyber incidents, EU Cyber Diplomacy Toolbox.

PESCO Cyber Projects: Spain participates in Permanent Structured Cooperation cyber projects: Cyber Rapid Response Teams (CRRT) — 8 nations share deployable cyber incident response capacity. Spain lead nation for "Cyber Threats and Incident Response Information Sharing Platform."

Five Eyes Observer: Spain not Five Eyes but receives selected SIGINT via bilateral agreements (US-Spain SOFA arrangements, NATO ATOMAL sharing). Post-Snowden: Spain demanded guarantees NSA not collecting on Spanish citizens — eventually formalised in 2014 US-Spain intelligence agreement.
► 11-M 2004: 191 killed Madrid train bombings — catalysed EU CTG intelligence reform
► CyCLONe: Spain on rotation as EU cyber crisis coordination lead
► CRRT: 8-nation cyber rapid response, 200 operators available within 48h
► US-Spain intel agreement 2014: data collection limits post-Snowden
► ENISA HQ: Athens — but operational teams distributed, Spain hosts satellite office
SpainEU IntelligenceINTCENCTGENISA
◆ INTERPOL — GLOBAL CYBER CRIME COORDINATION
// INTERPOL
INTERPOL Overview
IGCI Cyber Complex
Major Cyber Operations
Digital Forensics Lab
// METHODS
Red Notices & Cyber
Private Sector Fusion
INTERPOL — International Criminal Police Organization
Lyon, France | 194 member countries
Mission: Facilitate international police cooperation. Cyber division: IGCI (Singapore). Not a police force — coordinates, shares intelligence, issues notices. Cannot arrest; members act on its intelligence.

Cyber Focus: BEC (Business Email Compromise), ransomware, online fraud, CSAM, darknet markets. Coordinates SWAT-style global simultaneous takedowns across jurisdictions where unilateral action would fail.

Notices System: Red Notice (arrest request), Blue (info request), Purple (modus operandi). Cyber Red Notices now issued for ransomware operators — issued against REvil, Cl0p members.
► 194 member countries — more than UN membership
► IGCI Singapore: 400+ cyber staff, opened 2015
► Budget: ~€170M/year
► Cyber threat intel shared via I-24/7 secure network
► Interpol notices DB: 75,000+ active Red Notices
INTERPOLCyber194 NationsLyonI-24/7
IGCI — Global Complex for Innovation
Singapore | Est. 2015 | Cyber Hub
IGCI Structure: INTERPOL's dedicated cyber HQ. Four pillars: Cybercrime Operations Support (live ops), Cyber Fusion Centre (threat intel), Cyber Capability Development (training), Digital Forensics Lab.

Cyber Fusion Centre: Real-time threat intelligence sharing between member countries and private sector partners (Trend Micro, Kaspersky, Fortinet, Group-IB, Palo Alto). Produces Cyber Threat Intelligence Reports classified to member states.

Project Citius: Rapid response framework — coordinate action within 24h of a major cyber incident. Used in response to WannaCry (2017), Exchange zero-days (2021), Log4j (2021).
► Private partners: Trend Micro, Kaspersky, Group-IB, Fortinet, Palo Alto, Symantec
► I-24/7 network: encrypted comms to all 194 NCBs (National Central Bureaus)
► Cyber Fusion Centre: 24/7 ops floor, classified threat feeds
► Gateway Programme: connects INTERPOL to private sector IOCs in real-time
► INTERPOL Academy: cyber training to 140+ countries annually
IGCISingaporeCyber FusionPrivate SectorTraining
Major INTERPOL Cyber Operations
Coordinated Global Takedowns
OPERATION GOLDFISH ALPHA (2019): Asia-Pacific router vulnerability campaign. 9 countries, 5 months. Fixed compromised routers used for cryptomining — 78% of identified vulnerable routers secured. 30,000+ devices.

OPERATION CYCLONE (2021): Ransomware — Cl0p group. 30-month operation. Ukraine arrests, infrastructure seized. US, Korea, Ukraine, France cooperation. First major coordinated global ransomware arrests.

OPERATION HAECHI I-IV (2021-2024): BEC, investment fraud, crypto fraud. HAECHI-IV (2023): 3,500 arrests, $300M seized, 34 countries. Targets: voice phishing (vishing), pig-butchering crypto scams, money mule networks.

OPERATION SYNERGIA (2023-2024): Phishing, ransomware, banking trojans. 60 countries, 294 servers seized, 1,300 suspicious IPs flagged. First coordinated C2 infrastructure disruption at INTERPOL scale.

OPERATION NERVONE (2023): Targeted Genesis Market criminal marketplace. Coordinated with FBI/Europol. 119 arrests, 208 property searches, ~500k bot credentials seized.
► Goldfish Alpha: 9 countries, 30,000 routers secured in 5 months
► HAECHI-IV: $300M seized in one operation — record for INTERPOL cyber
► Synergia: 60 countries simultaneous action — largest ever cyber coordination
► Cl0p arrests (Cyclone): 6 arrested Ukraine, infrastructure in multiple EU countries
INTERPOLOperationsTakedownRansomwareCl0p
INTERPOL Digital Forensics Lab
Singapore IGCI | Classified Capabilities
Capabilities: Mobile device forensics (Cellebrite UFED, Oxygen Forensic Detective), darknet investigations, cryptocurrency tracing, malware reverse engineering, cloud data preservation.

Cryptocurrency Tracing: INTERPOL uses Chainalysis Reactor, CipherTrace, Elliptic to trace crypto flows. Seize wallets by targeting exchanges in cooperative jurisdictions. Have traced Lazarus Group BTC flows across 12+ mixers.

DFIR Toolkit: Autopsy, Volatility, Cellebrite, UFED Cloud Analyzer, Axiom, Magnet AXIOM. Mobile: chip-off forensics for damaged devices. Memory forensics: Volatility plugins for extracting C2 configs from RAT memory.

Dark Web Investigations: Trained analysts use Tor, I2P, Freenet. HUMINT infiltration of criminal forums. Undercover operations on dark markets. Collaboration with Europol's EC3 for joint darknet ops.
► Cellebrite UFED: extracts data from 28,000+ mobile device models
► Chainalysis: identified $100M+ in Lazarus Group BTC across 12 mixers
► Chip-off: physical NAND flash extraction from damaged mobiles
► Memory forensics: extracted Cobalt Strike configs from live APT implants
► INTERPOL forensics training: 400+ investigators trained/year across 60 countries
INTERPOLForensicsCellebriteCrypto TracingDark Web
Red Notices & Cyber Criminal Fugitives
Interpol Notice System Applied to Cybercrime
How It Works: Member country submits Red Notice request. INTERPOL General Secretariat validates (legal standards, political neutrality). Published globally to 194 NCBs. Not an international arrest warrant — member countries choose to act.

Cyber Red Notices: Issued for: ransomware operators (REvil, Conti affiliates), BEC ring leaders, state-sponsored hackers (US DoJ unseals indictments → INTERPOL Red Notice). Evgeny Bogachev (GameOver Zeus/$3M FBI reward) has Red Notice — lives in Russia, untouched.

The Russia/China Problem: Red Notices useless if suspect is in non-cooperating country. Russia and China do not extradite own nationals. Only effective when target travels internationally — FSB officer arrested in Spain 2017 on Red Notice, immediately released after Russian pressure.

Effective Enforcement: Most effective against cybercriminals who travel: Maksim Yakubets (Evil Corp) travels to Dubai — UAE cooperates. Lazarus members cannot travel to any cooperative country. This containment strategy limits operational mobility.
► 75,000+ active Red Notices globally; ~1,000 cyber-related
► Bogachev: $3M FBI reward, Red Notice — safe in Anapa, Russia since 2014
► Yakubets (Evil Corp): $5M US reward, Red Notice — travels only to Russia/Belarus
► REvil arrests: 14 arrested Russia 2022 (brief) — released after Ukraine invasion
► INTERPOL notices: non-binding, but trigger border alerts in 194 countries
Red NoticeFugitivesRussiaChinaExtradition
Private Sector Fusion — Gateway Programme
Threat Intel Sharing at Scale
Project Gateway: INTERPOL's formal private-sector partnership. Companies join Gateway to share: malware samples, IOCs (IPs, domains, hashes), TTPs, victim data (anonymised). In return: law enforcement action, classified threat briefings.

Partners: Trend Micro, Kaspersky Lab, Fortinet, Group-IB, Palo Alto Networks, Symantec/Broadcom, Microsoft (MSTIC), Amazon (AWS Abuse), Cloudflare, Chainalysis. Each has dedicated INTERPOL liaison.

How IOCs Flow: Private company detects campaign → shares IOCs via Gateway portal → INTERPOL Cyber Fusion Centre enriches → disseminates to relevant NCBs → country-level law enforcement acts. Typical: 48-72h from discovery to actionable police intelligence.

Successes: Trend Micro sharing led to SilverTerrier (Nigerian BEC) arrests. Group-IB intelligence underpinned Operation Goldfish Alpha. Kaspersky threat reports directly enabled Operation Cyclone (Cl0p) coordination.
► Gateway members: 30+ private companies, 194 law enforcement agencies
► IOC sharing: ~50,000 malicious indicators shared/month via Gateway
► 48-72h: average time from private sector report to police action
► Trend Micro + INTERPOL: co-authored public threat reports on SilverTerrier
► Kaspersky cooperation: continued despite US government ban on Kaspersky products
GatewayPrivate SectorIOC SharingTrend MicroGroup-IB
◆ EUROPOL — EC3 CYBER CRIME CENTRE
// EUROPOL
Europol Overview
EC3 Cybercrime Centre
J-CAT Operations
Major Takedowns
// INTELLIGENCE
Dark Web Operations
ECTEG Training
Europol — European Union Agency for Law Enforcement Cooperation
The Hague, Netherlands | Est. 1999
Mission: Support 27 EU member states + partners (US FBI, Australian AFP, RCMP etc.) in fighting serious organised crime and terrorism. Unlike INTERPOL: can deploy officers alongside national police, runs joint investigation teams (JITs).

SIENA Network: Secure Information Exchange Network Application. EU law enforcement encrypted comms. 50,000+ users across 49 countries. Like I-24/7 for EU specifically — real-time case data, intelligence, alerts.

EC3: European Cybercrime Centre (2013). Europol's dedicated cyber arm. Three focus areas: child sexual abuse material (CSAM), payment card fraud/carding, cyber attacks against critical infrastructure.

Regulation: Europol Regulation 2016/794 — can process big data, use predictive analytics, run operational analysis. 2022 update: expanded database sharing powers. HQ: Eisenhowerlaan, The Hague.
► 1,500+ Europol staff, 200+ liaison officers from 50+ countries
► SIENA: 50,000+ users, encrypted, available to all EU law enforcement
► EC3: 100+ cyber specialists, runs 24/7 ops floor
► JITs: 200+ joint investigation teams/year involving Europol support
► Budget: €220M (2024) — doubled since 2016
EuropolEC3EUThe HagueSIENA
EC3 — European Cybercrime Centre
Europol HQ | Est. 2013
Three Focal Areas:
1. Cyber Attacks (Focus Point CYBORG): ransomware, DDoS, malware campaigns against EU CNI. Tracks APT groups, coordinates response with national CERTs, ENISA.
2. Online Fraud (Focus Point TERMINAL): payment card fraud, carding shops, skimming. Runs EMPACT (European Multidisciplinary Platform Against Criminal Threats).
3. CSAM (Focus Point TWINS): Child sexual abuse material — tracks, identifies, coordinates removals and arrests globally.

Operational Analysis: EC3 analysts embed in national cybercrime units during major ops. Example: 4 EC3 analysts co-located in FBI Cyber Division during Genesis Market takedown.

ENISA Partnership: EC3 + ENISA publish annual IOCTA (Internet Organised Crime Threat Assessment) — authoritative EU cybercrime landscape report. Drives EU policy on cyber law enforcement.
► CYBORG: coordinates against APT28, Sandworm, Fancy Lazarus ransomware ops
► TERMINAL: 300,000+ compromised cards recovered from carding markets 2023
► IOCTA 2024: ransomware remains #1 threat, 15% YoY increase in attacks
► EC3 analysts: 50+ nationalities, 15+ languages spoken
► Focus Point meetings: quarterly, classified, 27 EU NCAs + FBI/AFP guests
EC3EuropolCYBORGIOCTACyber Attacks
J-CAT — Joint Cybercrime Action Taskforce
EC3 | Operational Cyber Strike Team
What Is J-CAT: Permanent operational team inside EC3, The Hague. Liaison officers from: FBI, NCA (UK), BKA (Germany), RCMP (Canada), AFP (Australia), and 8+ EU member state cybercrime units — all co-located in one room.

Mission: Facilitate and coordinate simultaneous law enforcement action across multiple countries on a single cyber criminal operation. Cuts through the traditional months-long MLAT (Mutual Legal Assistance Treaty) bureaucracy.

How Operations Run: J-CAT receives intelligence → builds shared operational picture → coordinates simultaneous action across countries on D-Day. Each national officer can directly call their home country's cyber units without going through formal channels.

Successes: Directly ran Genesis Market (2023), Hive ransomware (2023 — FBI led, J-CAT coordinated EU side), LockBit (Feb 2024 — 10 countries, J-CAT central coordination), BreachForums (2024).
► J-CAT members: FBI, NCA, BKA, RCMP, AFP + 8 EU units in one office
► Genesis Market 2023: 208 searches, 119 arrests, 17 countries — J-CAT coordinated
► LockBit Feb 2024: 10 countries, 2 arrests, 34 servers seized — J-CAT centre
► Hive (2023): FBI infiltrated infrastructure for 7 months, J-CAT shared intel to EU victims
► MLAT bypass: J-CAT liaison officers bypass 6-12 month formal request timelines
J-CATEuropolOperationsFBILockBit
Major Europol Cyber Takedowns
EC3 / J-CAT Operations
EMOTET (Jan 2021): Most dangerous botnet at time. 8 countries simultaneous action. 700+ C2 servers seized across Ukraine, Netherlands, Germany, UK, France, Lithuania, Canada, US. Malware uninstaller pushed to infected machines via Emotet's own C2 on 25 April 2021. 4 arrests Ukraine.

GENESIS MARKET (Apr 2023): Cybercriminal marketplace selling "bots" — stolen credentials + browser fingerprints allowing account takeover. 2M+ victims. 119 arrested, 208 property searches, 17 countries, 208 domains seized. FBI + J-CAT + 16 EU nations.

LOCKBIT (Feb 2024 — CRONOS): Most prolific ransomware group. UK NCA led. 10 countries, 2 arrests (Poland, Ukraine), 34 servers, 1,000 decryption keys recovered, $110M in BTC traced. Europol published LockBit operators' real identities on seized website.

RAGNAR LOCKER (Oct 2023): Main suspect arrested in Paris. Infrastructure seized in 11 countries. Notable: Europol published arrested suspect's face on takedown site — psychological deterrent.

DARKSIDE/BLACKMATTER: Colonial Pipeline group. FBI seized $2.3M BTC from affiliate wallet. Europol coordinated EU-side intelligence that identified Eastern European affiliates.
► Emotet: uninstaller pushed via C2 — unique technique, cleaned 1M+ machines
► Genesis Market: 208 simultaneous searches across 17 countries on one morning
► LockBit: seized site showed countdown timer then revealed operator identities
► Ragnar Locker: arrested at Paris CDG airport, servers seized across 11 EU nations
► CRONOS: UK NCA named operation — LockBit admin Dmitry Khoroshev identified/sanctioned
EuropolEmotetLockBitGenesisTakedowns
Dark Web Operations — Europol EC3
Undercover & Technical Ops
Undercover Operations: EC3 trains officers for undercover darknet presence. Infiltrate criminal forums (BreachForums, RAMP, XSS.is) as buyers/sellers to build evidence. Legal framework varies by country — German BKA cannot pay for drugs as UCA; FBI can.

Infrastructure Takedowns: Technical approach — seize servers hosting darknet markets. Key technique: find the real IP behind Tor hidden service via: OPSEC failures (operator accessed clearnet from market server IP), malformed HTTP requests leaking server IP, correlation attacks (timing), or malware on admin panel.

How Criminals Are Caught: 1. Exit scam analysis — crypto wallet clustering
2. Forum registration metadata (email, creation IP)
3. Bitcoin tracing through exchanges requiring KYC
4. Seller shipping patterns + package interception
5. Operational slip-ups (posting on clearnet with same username)

HYDRA Takedown (2022): Largest Russian dark web market. $5B in lifetime crypto. BKA + DEA seized servers in Germany. 543 BTC seized (~$25M). Hydra had 19,000 vendor accounts, 17M customers.
► Hydra: BKA seized Frankfurt servers — found because Hydra used German hosting
► AlphaBay reboot: Europol monitored from day 1, arrested admin DeSnake in 2022
► Forum infiltration: EC3 agents active on XSS.is, Exploit.in, RAMP for months before arrests
► Bitcoin: Chainalysis traced $5B Hydra flows — identified top vendors via exchange KYC
► UCA rules: Europol coordinates legal framework differences between 27 member states
Dark WebEC3TorHydraUndercover
ECTEG — European Cybercrime Training
Capacity Building Across EU
What Is ECTEG: European Cybercrime Training and Education Group. Consortium of EU law enforcement training academies (CEPOL being the main one) coordinating cyber training across 27 member states.

Courses: Open Source Intelligence (OSINT), Dark Web investigations, malware analysis for LEO, mobile forensics, cryptocurrency tracing, network forensics, critical infrastructure protection, undercover online operations.

CEPOL CYBERPOL: European Police College online training platform. 500+ hours of cyber content. Free to EU law enforcement. Includes: practical labs (virtual machines), case studies from real Europol operations, assessment and certification.

Industry Partnerships: ECTEG partners with: Cellebrite (mobile forensics), Chainalysis (crypto tracing), Maltego (OSINT), Group-IB (malware analysis). These companies provide tools + training to EU law enforcement at no/reduced cost in exchange for partnership logos and referrals.
► CEPOL trains 30,000+ EU law enforcement officers/year across all disciplines
► Cyber courses: 40+ dedicated cybercrime titles, practical VM-based labs
► Chainalysis partnership: free Reactor licenses to EU police crypto investigators
► Maltego: OSINT tool, free to verified law enforcement globally
► ECTEG curriculum aligned with European Cybercrime Training Standard (ECTS)
ECTEGTrainingEUCEPOLCapacity Building
△ NATO — CYBER AS THE 5TH DOMAIN
// NATO
NATO Cyber Overview
CCDCOE Tallinn
Locked Shields
Article 5 & Cyber War
// OPERATIONS
NCIRC Cyber Defence
Hybrid Threats
NATO — Cyber as the 5th Domain
North Atlantic Treaty Organization | Est. 1949
Cyber Domain Declaration: Warsaw Summit 2016 — NATO formally recognised cyberspace as a domain of warfare alongside land, sea, air, space. This meant cyber attacks could, in principle, trigger Article 5 collective defence.

Key Bodies:
► CCDCOE: Cooperative Cyber Defence Centre of Excellence (Tallinn, Estonia) — research + training
► NCIRC: NATO Computer Incident Response Capability — operational cyber defence
► CYBERCOM Alliance: Coordination with US Cyber Command
► NCI Agency: NATO Communications and Information Agency — network ops

Tallinn Manual: Non-binding academic guide on how international law applies to cyber operations. Created by 20 international law experts at CCDCOE. Establishes: when cyber attack = "use of force," when states can respond with kinetic force, what is a "cyber armed attack."
► 32 Allies + 8 contributing nations (including Ukraine, Japan, Australia)
► Cyber pledge: all NATO nations commit to improving national cyber defences
► Warsaw 2016: cyber = 5th domain. Brussels 2021: more explicit Article 5 cyber language
► CCDCOE: 39 contributing nations, 200+ staff, classified research facility
► NCI Agency: operates 30,000+ NATO network nodes, cyber defence 24/7
NATO5th DomainCyber WarWarsawAlliance
CCDCOE — Cooperative Cyber Defence Centre of Excellence
Tallinn, Estonia | Est. 2008
Mission: NATO-accredited research, training, and exercises hub for cyber defence. NOT an operational cyber command. Produces research, trains NATO cyber officers, runs Locked Shields (world's largest cyber exercise).

Tallinn Manual 2.0 (2017): 600-page authoritative guide on cyber war law. Key rulings:
► Cyber operation = "use of force" if scale/effects comparable to kinetic attack
► Cyber attack killing people or destroying critical infrastructure = "armed attack" → triggers Art. 5
► States can use force in self-defence to stop ongoing cyber attack
► Espionage in cyberspace is NOT prohibited by international law

Research Output: Annual Cyber Threats Report, Hybrid CoE partnership publications, attribution framework guidance, cyber norms recommendations. All public except Annex materials.

Blue/Red Teams: CCDCOE runs permanent red team capability for Allied exercises. Staff include ex-NSA, ex-GCHQ, ex-NCSC officers seconded by member nations.
► 39 contributing nations: all NATO + Australia, Japan, Ukraine, Israel, South Korea
► Tallinn Manual: created 2013, updated 2017 — 154 "black letter rules" of cyber warfare
► Budget: classified, funded by contributing nations (proportional to GDP)
► Staff: ~200 permanent + rotating national secondees
► Annual conference: CyCon Tallinn — 500+ attendees, classified + unclassified tracks
CCDCOETallinnEstoniaInternational LawResearch
Locked Shields — World's Largest Cyber Exercise
CCDCOE Annual | Tallinn, Estonia
What Is Locked Shields: Real-time network defence exercise. 2,000+ cyber defenders from 30+ countries defend simulated national infrastructure against a red team of 300+ expert attackers. Ran annually since 2010 — now the gold standard for national cyber readiness testing.

2024 Exercise: 4,000 virtualised systems, 5,000 attack vectors, military + civilian systems simulated. Nations defend: military C2 networks, power grids, financial systems, satellite comms, water treatment. Simultaneously test: technical defence, legal decision-making, strategic communication, political decisions.

Red Team: 300+ volunteers from CCDCOE member nations. Tasked to compromise as many systems as possible. Use real TTPs: APT28 techniques, living-off-the-land, supply chain attacks. Blue teams must detect, respond, report — all scored in real-time.

Scoring: Technical (systems kept up), situational awareness (accurate reporting), legal (correct decisions on response), forensics (accurate attribution). Estonia, Finland, US consistently top. Ukraine: participated 2022 WHILE under real Russian cyber attack.
► Locked Shields 2024: 40 Blue Teams, 4,000 virtual systems, 30 nations
► Red team: uses actual nation-state TTPs — Sandworm, APT28 methods replicated
► Legal track: teams must decide if response actions are legal under IHL
► Ukraine 2022: participated in exercise WHILE defending real Russian Sandworm attacks
► Winner 2023: Finland team — credited to mandatory national cyber reserve programme
Locked ShieldsExerciseCCDCOERed TeamDefence
Article 5 & Cyber Warfare
Collective Defence in the Cyber Domain
The Question: Article 5 — attack on one is attack on all. Does a cyber attack trigger it? NATO's answer: "Yes, a cyber attack could trigger Article 5" — but deliberately vague on threshold. Why? Deterrence requires ambiguity.

Brussels 2021 Communiqué: First explicit language that a cyber attack could trigger Article 5. "The impact of significant malicious cumulative cyber activities might, in certain circumstances, be considered as amounting to an armed attack." Cumulative = multiple smaller attacks together.

Attribution Challenge: Article 5 invocation requires political consensus of all 32 Allies on attribution. Hard to achieve when states disagree on attribution confidence. WannaCry: UK/US attributed to DPRK but 3 NATO allies refused to sign joint statement.

Cyber Solidarity: Short of Art. 5 — NATO has "Cyber Solidarity" where members assist attacked ally with: forensics support, incident response, threat intelligence. Used for Montenegro (2016), Albania (2022 Iranian attack), Montenegro (2022 Cuba ransomware).

Hack Back Debate: Can NATO collectively conduct offensive cyber operations? Officially: individual nations retain sovereign right. CYBERCOM Hunt Forward teams deploy to Allied nations offensively (Estonia, Lithuania, Montenegro confirmed).
► Article 5 invoked once ever: post-9/11 (kinetic, 2001)
► Cyber threshold: no defined threshold — deliberate strategic ambiguity
► CYBERCOM Hunt Forward: US deploys offensive cyber teams to Allied nations with consent
► Montenegro 2022: Cuba ransomware took down govt — NATO Cyber Solidarity activated
► Ukraine: not Article 5 eligible (not NATO member) — but Allies provide offensive tools
Article 5Cyber WarCollective DefenceAttributionCYBERCOM
NCIRC — NATO Computer Incident Response Capability
Mons, Belgium | 24/7 Cyber Defence
Mission: Protect NATO networks, C2 systems, and classified infrastructure from cyber attack. 24/7 monitoring and incident response. NOT national network defence — that is each nation's responsibility.

What It Defends: 30,000+ NATO network nodes across 32 nations. CRONOS (NATO strategic communications), BICES (intelligence sharing network), LOCE (classified ops network), NIPRNet equivalent for Alliance.

Threat Landscape: NATO networks attacked 500M+ times/day (Director NCI Agency estimate). Primary threats: Russian APT28/Sandworm, Chinese Volt Typhoon (since 2023), Iranian Charming Kitten. Significant volume from criminal actors testing defences.

Incident Response: Tiered response: Tier 1 (NCIRC handles), Tier 2 (NCI Agency crisis response), Tier 3 (Secretary General convenes NAC — North Atlantic Council). SolarWinds 2020: NATO activated Tier 2, audited all Orion installations in NATO infrastructure within 72h.
► 500M+ attack attempts/day on NATO networks (NCI Agency 2023 report)
► NATO Cyber Operations Centre (CYOC): operational cyber command, Brussels
► BICES: NATO intelligence sharing network — hardened against APT espionage
► SolarWinds: NATO declared clean within 2 weeks — 4 NATO systems affected but contained
► NCIRC staff: 200+ analysts, intelligence officers, red/blue team members
NCIRCNATOIncident ResponseDefence24/7
Hybrid Threats — Cyber + Everything Else
StratCom, Disinformation, Electronic Warfare
Hybrid Warfare Definition: Simultaneous use of conventional military, cyber, information operations, economic coercion, and proxy forces below the threshold of declared war. Russia's Gerasimov Doctrine (misnamed) — "new generation warfare."

Cyber Component: In hybrid campaigns, cyber is used for: intelligence collection pre-kinetic, degrading adversary C2 ahead of conventional forces, psychological ops (leak documents to demoralise), infrastructure disruption (power grids before ground advance). Ukraine 2022: Viasat KA-SAT attack 1 hour before Russian ground forces crossed border.

STRATCOM: NATO Strategic Communications Centre of Excellence (Riga, Latvia). Counters disinformation, detects influence operations, trains Allies in narrative warfare. Monitors: Russian state media, China's FIMI, social media manipulation campaigns.

EW + Cyber Convergence: Modern warfare: GPS jamming (Russia in Baltic) + cyber attack on air traffic control + disinformation = multi-domain denial. NATO developing "multi-domain operations" doctrine to integrate cyber with EW, space, land, sea.
► Viasat attack: 1h before Russian ground invasion — C2 disruption for Ukraine forces
► Baltic GPS jamming: 1,800+ incidents 2023 — Russian EW affecting Finnish/Estonian airspace
► NATO STRATCOM Riga: tracks 3,000+ disinformation narratives/month
► Sandworm + GRU cyber preceded every major Russian kinetic action in Ukraine
► Ukraine: world's largest live testing ground for hybrid cyber-kinetic warfare integration
HybridNATODisinformationSTRATCOMUkraine

🛡
SENTINEL COMMAND
CRITICAL INFRASTRUCTURE DEFENSE
THREATCON BRAVO
SESSION 00:00:00
SENTINEL ONLINE
🤖
SENTINEL // defensive intelligence — sixteen sectors, one mission
◆ CISA — CYBERSECURITY & INFRASTRUCTURE SECURITY
// CISA
CISA Overview
KEV Catalog
Joint Advisories
#StopRansomware
// PROGRAMMES
JCDC — Private Sector
Secure by Design
CISA — Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency
Washington D.C. | Est. 2018 | ~3,000 staff
Mission: Lead national effort to understand, manage, and reduce risk to US cyber and physical critical infrastructure. Created from DHS/NPPD by Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency Act 2018 — upgraded from office to standalone agency.

16 Critical Infrastructure Sectors: Chemical, Commercial Facilities, Communications, Critical Manufacturing, Dams, Defence Industrial Base, Emergency Services, Energy, Financial Services, Food/Ag, Government, Healthcare, IT, Nuclear, Transportation, Water/Wastewater. Each has a Sector Risk Management Agency (SRMA).

Key Functions: Threat intelligence sharing (TLP), vulnerability management (KEV catalog), incident response support (free to CI operators), cyber exercises (Cyber Storm, GridEx), workforce development, election security.

Director: Jen Easterly (2021-2025) transformed CISA from compliance agency to operational one. Coined "Secure by Design." Successor: Sean Plankey. CISA director requires Senate confirmation.
► 3,000+ staff, 10 regional offices across US
► Budget: $3B (FY2024) — doubled since 2018
► CISA Alert AA series: public advisories on active threats, co-authored with FBI/NSA
► Automated Indicator Sharing (AIS): 300+ organisations receive real-time IOCs
► CISA responds to 1,000+ significant cyber incidents/year on federal networks
CISAUSCritical InfrastructureDHS16 Sectors
KEV — Known Exploited Vulnerabilities Catalog
The Most Important Vulnerability List in the World
What Is KEV: CISA's catalog of CVEs confirmed to be actively exploited in the wild. Launched November 2021. US federal agencies MUST patch KEV entries within mandated timeframes (2 weeks for critical, 6 months for others). Over 1,100 CVEs as of 2024.

Why It Matters: Most CVE lists have 200,000+ entries — impossible to prioritise. KEV cuts this to ~1,100 confirmed exploited. Red teams: check target systems against KEV first — if they haven't patched these, you have a very high probability of initial access.

Criteria for Addition: CVE exists + evidence of active exploitation in wild + clear guidance exists. CISA adds entries within days of exploitation evidence. Sources: threat intel, OSINT, partner agencies, incident response reports.

Offensive Use of KEV: State actors like Volt Typhoon target KEV entries specifically on network edge devices (VPN appliances, routers, firewalls) — knowing these are underpatched in CNI. KEV entries on Ivanti, Citrix, Fortinet, Cisco are highest risk.

EPSS vs CVSS: CVSS = severity score (1-10 static). EPSS = Exploit Prediction Scoring System (0-100%, updates daily). CISA recommends EPSS + KEV together. High EPSS + on KEV = patch TODAY.
► KEV entries: 1,100+ CVEs (Nov 2021 - present) all confirmed exploited
► Federal mandate: agencies MUST patch KEV items — OPM verifies compliance
► Most exploited 2023: MOVEit (CVE-2023-34362), Citrix Bleed, Ivanti EPMM
► KEV API: cisa.gov/sites/default/files/feeds/known_exploited_vulnerabilities.json
► Volt Typhoon: specifically targets KEV entries on OT/CNI network edge devices
KEVCISAVulnerabilityPatchingMandate
Joint Cyber Security Advisories
NSA + FBI + CISA + GCHQ + ASD + CCCS + NCSC
The Five Eyes + Format: CISA co-authors advisories with: NSA, FBI, NCSC (UK), ASD (Australia), CCCS (Canada), GCSB (NZ), DGSI (France), BSI (Germany). Multi-nation endorsement = highest confidence attribution and technical detail.

Advisory Types:
► CSA (Cybersecurity Advisory): specific threat actor/campaign
► MAR (Malware Analysis Report): reverse-engineered malware IOCs + YARA
► AA (Alert): urgent active exploitation
► TLP:CLEAR: public. TLP:GREEN: partners. TLP:AMBER: sector. TLP:RED: org only

Notable Advisories: AA22-321A (Iranian APT + ransomware), AA23-165A (Volt Typhoon LOTL TTPs — most comprehensive Chinese APT advisory ever published), AA24-038A (Ivanti Connect Secure mass exploitation), AA22-074A (Russian actors targeting CNI — water, energy, comms).

Reading a CISA Advisory: Executive Summary → Technical Details (TTPs in ATT&CK format) → IOCs (hashes, IPs, domains, YARA) → Detection (Sigma rules, Splunk queries) → Mitigations (specific product configs). Format standardised since 2021.
► AA23-165A Volt Typhoon: 50+ pages, most detailed Chinese APT TTPs ever published
► Joint advisories: up to 10 nations co-signing — unprecedented intelligence sharing
► IOC feeds: CISA shares machine-readable IOCs via STIX/TAXII and AIS platform
► YARA rules: all CISA MARs include YARA for malware detection
► Advisory lag: typically 30-90 days behind discovery — time for victims to remediate first
CISAJoint AdvisoryNSAFive EyesATT&CK
#StopRansomware — CISA Initiative
Whole-of-Government Ransomware Response
Launch: April 2021 following Colonial Pipeline. Centralised hub for all US government ransomware resources. stopransomware.gov — advisories specific to ransomware groups, technical details, victim guidance, report mechanisms.

Advisory Format: Each #StopRansomware advisory covers: group background, TTPs in ATT&CK format, IOCs (all known hashes/domains/IPs), initial access vectors used, specific hardening steps, DFIR guidance. Example: LockBit 3.0 advisory — 20 pages, 200+ IOCs, 50 ATT&CK techniques.

RaaS Economics Coverage: Advisories now address: affiliate structures, double extortion (encrypt + leak), negotiation tactics, cryptocurrency payment methods, how to NOT pay (decryptors available), legal considerations of paying ransoms (OFAC sanctions).

Ransomware Vulnerability Warning Pilot (RVWP): CISA proactively notifies organisations it detects have known vulnerabilities exploited by ransomware groups. Sent 7,000+ notifications to critical infrastructure operators in 2023. Free service.
► RVWP: 7,117 notifications to CI operators in 2023 — free proactive service
► stopransomware.gov: 60+ group-specific advisories as of 2024
► Payment ban debate: CISA Director supports outlawing ransom payments — bill pending Senate
► OFAC sanctions: paying ransomed to sanctioned group = illegal, OFAC enforcement
► LockBit advisory: 200+ IOCs, 47 ATT&CK techniques — most comprehensive ransomware advisory
RansomwareCISAStopRansomwareLockBitColonial Pipeline
JCDC — Joint Cyber Defense Collaborative
Government + Private Sector Fusion
What Is JCDC: Created August 2021. Permanent collaboration between CISA and private sector to jointly plan, exercise, and respond to major cyber incidents. Not advisory — operational. First time private companies formally integrated into US cyber defence planning.

Members: Microsoft, Google, Amazon (AWS), Meta, Cloudflare, AT&T, Verizon, Mandiant (now Google), Palo Alto Networks, CrowdStrike, Lumen, Cisco. Plus: FBI, NSA, CYBERCOM, sector ISACs, Five Eyes partners. ~300 organisations.

Operations: JCDC activated for: Log4j (2021 — coordinated global response within 72h of disclosure), Ukraine pre-invasion (JCDC pre-positioned defensive measures with Ukrainian networks), Colonial Pipeline post-mortem, SolarWinds remediation guidance.

Information Sharing: Members share classified (up to SECRET) and unclassified threat intelligence in both directions. CISA provides: government threat intel (NSA feeds), attribution intelligence. Private sector provides: telemetry at scale (Microsoft sees 65 trillion signals/day).
► JCDC members: 300+ orgs including Big Tech, telcos, MSSPs, ISACs
► Microsoft threat intel: 65 trillion security signals/day — shared with JCDC
► Log4j 2021: JCDC coordinated global patching guidance within 72h of public disclosure
► Ukraine pre-invasion: JCDC briefed Ukrainian networks 2 weeks before Feb 24 attack
► Clearance: key JCDC private sector staff hold SECRET clearances for classified briefings
JCDCCISAPrivate SectorMicrosoftThreat Intel
Secure by Design / Secure by Default
CISA's Software Manufacturer Accountability Push
The Shift: Traditional cybersecurity puts burden on users to configure security correctly. Secure by Design: software manufacturers must ship products that are secure OUT OF THE BOX. Default configurations must not be exploitable. Memory-safe languages by default.

Principles:
1. Default security: MFA on by default, no default passwords, logging on by default
2. Eliminate classes of vulnerabilities: memory-safe languages (Rust, Go, Python) over C/C++
3. Transparency: CVD policy, public vulnerability disclosure, software bills of materials (SBOM)

Memory Safety Push: 70% of CVEs in Microsoft, Google, NSA products are memory safety issues (buffer overflows, use-after-free, etc.). NSA/CISA/GCHQ/ASD joint guidance: migrate from C/C++ to Rust, Go, Swift, Python, Java, C# for new development.

Pledges: 200+ software manufacturers signed Secure by Design pledge 2024. Commitments include: measurable goals for MFA adoption on products, CVD policies, reducing entire classes of vulnerabilities within 1 year. Public tracking dashboard.
► 70% of CVEs: memory safety issues — root cause of most exploitation
► Secure by Design pledge: 200+ companies signed, including Microsoft, Google, Cisco
► SBOM mandate: Executive Order 14028 requires SBOM for federal software procurement
► NSA memory safety guidance: recommends Rust/Go over C/C++ for all new DoD development
► CISA 2030 goal: 50% reduction in exploited vulnerabilities in critical infrastructure
Secure by DesignCISAMemory SafetyRustSBOM
{%- raw -%}
◆ SIRP — SISTEMA DE INFORMAÇÕES DA REPÚBLICA PORTUGUESA
// PORTUGAL
SIRP Overview
SIS — Domestic Intel
SIED — Foreign Intel
CNCS — Cyber Authority
// TRADECRAFT
Legal Limits & Oversight
Notable Cases
EU / NATO Cooperation
SIRP — Sistema de Informações da República Portuguesa
Lisbon | Est. 1984 (Lei 30/84) | civilian intelligence system
Portugal's umbrella intelligence system, coordinating the country's two civilian intelligence services. Headed by a Secretary-General (SGSIRP) who reports to the Prime Minister. Unlike most states, Portugal splits intelligence strictly between a domestic and a foreign service and keeps both firmly civilian — military intelligence (DIMIL) sits separately under the Armed Forces.

Two services: SIS (Serviço de Informações de Segurança — domestic security) and SIED (Serviço de Informações Estratégicas de Defesa — external/strategic). Both share the SGSIRP support structure (common HR, legal, data systems) under Lei 9/2007.

Defining feature: Portuguese intelligence officers have no police powers — they cannot arrest, search, or lawfully intercept communications themselves. Intelligence is handed to the Polícia Judiciária or the Public Prosecutor for any coercive action. Classic covert HUMINT "agent-running" is tightly constrained by law.

Oversight: CFSIRP (Conselho de Fiscalização do SIRP), a council elected by Parliament, reviews legality; a separate data-oversight commission of judges supervises personal-data processing.
► Legal basis: Lei 30/84 (Lei-Quadro do SIRP), amended by Lei 9/2007 (SG structure)
► Reports to: Prime Minister via the Secretary-General (SGSIRP)
► No police powers — Lei 30/84 forbids officers from arrests, searches or wiretaps
► Military intelligence (DIMIL) is separate, under the Estado-Maior-General das Forças Armadas
► Oversight: CFSIRP (parliamentary council) + Comissão de Fiscalização de Dados (judges)
SIRPPortugalLisbonEUNATO
SIS — Serviço de Informações de Segurança
Lisbon | domestic security intelligence
Portugal's internal security intelligence service — the rough equivalent of the UK's MI5 or Germany's BfV. Mission: produce intelligence to safeguard internal security and prevent sabotage, terrorism, espionage and acts that threaten the constitutional order on Portuguese territory.

Focus areas: counter-terrorism (jihadist recruitment and transit, violent extremism), counter-espionage against foreign services operating in Portugal, threats to critical infrastructure, and intelligence support on serious organised crime.

Limits: SIS analysts collect and assess — they do not investigate as police. Findings are passed to the Polícia Judiciária (PJ), the Serviço de Estrangeiros (border/immigration matters historically), or the Public Prosecutor. SIS cannot detain or interrogate suspects.
► Analogous to: MI5 (UK), BfV (Germany), DGSI (France)
► Coordinates with PJ's counter-terrorism unit (UNCT) and EUROPOL
► Member of the Counter Terrorism Group (CTG) — EU+ informal CT intelligence sharing
SISDomesticCounter-TerrorCounter-Espionage
SIED — Serviço de Informações Estratégicas de Defesa
Lisbon | foreign / strategic intelligence
Portugal's external intelligence service — broadly comparable to the UK's MI6 or France's DGSE, but without a covert-action / paramilitary mandate. Mission: produce strategic intelligence on external threats relevant to national defence, foreign policy and the security of the Portuguese state and its interests abroad.

Priorities: the Lusophone world (Angola, Mozambique, Cabo Verde, Guinea-Bissau, São Tomé, Timor-Leste and ties with Brazil), the Maghreb and Sahel, maritime security in the Atlantic, counter-proliferation, and economic/strategic intelligence within the EU and NATO frameworks.

Style: SIED is an analysis-and-liaison service. It relies heavily on partner-service exchange and open/diplomatic reporting rather than aggressive clandestine networks — a reflection of Portugal's legal constraints and its small-state posture.
► Analogous to: MI6 (UK), DGSE (France), CNI foreign arm (Spain) — minus covert action
► Strong regional lens on Lusophone Africa and the South Atlantic
► Feeds Portuguese input into EU INTCEN strategic assessments
SIEDForeignStrategicLusophone
CNCS — Centro Nacional de Cibersegurança
Lisbon | Est. 2014 | national cyber authority
Portugal's National Cybersecurity Centre, operating under the GNS (Gabinete Nacional de Segurança — the National Security Office that also manages NATO/EU classified-information credentials). CNCS is the national cybersecurity authority and operational CSIRT — a defensive body, not an offensive cyber agency.

Roles: runs CERT.PT (the national computer emergency response team), coordinates incident response across government and critical operators, sets the national cyberspace security strategy, and is the competent authority for transposed EU rules (NIS / NIS2) covering essential and important entities.

Ecosystem: works with sector regulators, the Rede Nacional CSIRT (national network of CSIRTs), academia and the private sector; issues advisories and runs awareness and skills programmes.
► Parent body: GNS (Gabinete Nacional de Segurança)
► Operates CERT.PT national CSIRT; member of the EU CSIRTs Network and FIRST
► Competent authority for NIS2 transposition (essential / important entities)
► Cooperates with ENISA and CISA-equivalent partners on advisories & exercises
CNCSCERT.PTDefensiveNIS2
Legal Limits & Oversight
Why Portugal's model is unusual in Europe
Portugal deliberately built one of Europe's most legally restrained intelligence frameworks, a reaction to the political-police abuses of the pre-1974 Estado Novo dictatorship (the notorious PIDE/DGS secret police). The 1974 Carnation Revolution made tight civilian control a constitutional priority.

No police powers: Lei 30/84 bars SIS/SIED officers from arrests, searches and interception of communications. They are an intelligence-only function; coercive measures belong to the courts and judicial police.

Metadata fight: in 2022 the Constitutional Court (Acórdão 268/2022) struck down Portugal's bulk telecom data-retention law as unconstitutional, forcing a redesign of how communications metadata can be retained and accessed — a recurring point of tension between security agencies and civil-liberties safeguards.

Oversight stack: CFSIRP (a council whose members are elected by Parliament) checks the legality of service activity; a commission of judges supervises personal-data files; the Prime Minister bears political responsibility.
► Historical context: PIDE/DGS secret police abolished after the 1974 revolution
► Lei 30/84 art. 4 — intelligence services explicitly hold no police authority
► Acórdão 268/2022 — Constitutional Court voided bulk data-retention regime
► CFSIRP publishes periodic public reports on legality of operations
OversightConstitutionCivil Liberties
Notable Cases & Debates
Public-record controversies and reforms
Portuguese intelligence rarely features in spectacular operations — by design — but it has been at the centre of recurring institutional debates, all well documented in the public record:

► Access-to-metadata debate: repeated legislative attempts to give SIS/SIED lawful access to telecom metadata, blocked or narrowed on constitutional grounds (culminating in Acórdão 268/2022).
► "Segredo de Estado": periodic political disputes over what may be classified as a state secret and withheld from Parliament.
► SIRP reform cycles: governments have repeatedly proposed merging or restructuring SIS and SIED, and clarifying the SGSIRP's authority, with mixed results.
► Document-security incidents: as in any service, handling and storage of classified material has occasionally drawn parliamentary scrutiny.

This panel summarises public, reported matters only — institutional and legal in nature, not operational tradecraft.
Public RecordReformParliament
EU / NATO Cooperation
Portugal in the alliance and union frameworks
Portugal is a founding member of NATO (1949) and an EU member state, and its intelligence and cyber bodies are wired into both ecosystems.

EU intelligence: Portugal contributes to EU INTCEN (the EU Intelligence and Situation Centre), participates in the Club de Berne and its Counter Terrorism Group (CTG), and SIS/SIED liaise bilaterally with European partner services.

Cyber: CNCS sits in the EU CSIRTs Network, cooperates with ENISA, and aligns Portuguese practice with EU NIS2 and the EU cyber crisis framework.

NATO: the GNS manages NATO/EU classified-information accreditation for Portugal. The Lajes Field air base in the Azores remains a strategically important mid-Atlantic facility used by NATO and US forces, and Portugal hosts NATO communications and maritime cooperation activities.
► NATO founding member (1949); EU member since 1986
► EU INTCEN, Club de Berne / CTG participation
► CNCS in EU CSIRTs Network; alignment with ENISA & NIS2
► Lajes Field (Azores) — strategic mid-Atlantic NATO/US base
NATOEU INTCENClub de BerneAzores
◆ TOP SECRET // DEFENSIVE OPERATIONS COMMAND // NOFORN ◆
🛡
USCYBERCOM · DEFENSIVE OPERATIONS
SECURITY OPERATIONS CENTER — WATCH FLOOR
THREATCON BRAVO
SHIFT 00:00:00
SENTINEL ONLINE
🤖
SENTINEL // defensive intelligence — at your service, Operator
◆ SOC ANALYST & INCIDENT RESPONSE — HOW PROS WORK
// OPERATIONS
◆ Inside the SOC
A Day in the SOC
Tier-1 Triage
The SOC Toolset
Tier-2 Investigation
// RESPONSE
Incident Response
Runbooks & Playbooks
Escalation & Comms
// PROGRAM
Metrics & KPIs
Threat Intelligence
Roles & Careers
Exercises & Improvement
// HANDS-ON
▶ Triage Simulator
▶ My Scorecard
▶ Playbook Library
Inside the SOC — Who Does What
The team that defends the org 24/7
THE SOC
🎯 HANDS-ON LAB:
What a SOC is: a Security Operations Center is the team (and often a physical watch floor) that monitors, detects, investigates and responds to cyber threats around the clock. Its core mission: reduce dwell time — the gap between when an attacker gets in and when they are caught and removed.

THE TIERED MODEL
Tier 1 — Triage Analyst: works the alert queue, validates alerts, closes false positives, escalates real ones. The front line.
Tier 2 — Incident Responder: deeper investigation, scoping, containment, handles escalated incidents.
Tier 3 — Threat Hunter / Forensics: proactive hunting, malware analysis, complex IR, advanced forensics.
Support roles: Detection Engineer (writes/tunes the rules), CTI Analyst (threat intel), SOC Manager (runs the floor).

SOC MODELS
In-house SOC · MSSP (outsourced monitoring) · MDR (managed detection & response) · Hybrid · Virtual SOC. Coverage is usually 24/7 via shift rotations or follow-the-sun across global sites.
A Day in the SOC
The analyst workflow, shift to shift
WORKFLOW
Analysts live in the queue. A typical shift is a loop of triage, document, escalate, repeat — bookended by handovers.

SHIFT RHYTHM
1. Handover / pass-down — read the previous shift's notes: open incidents, things to watch.
2. Health check — are all log sources reporting? Any SIEM/sensor outages? (Blind spots = risk.)
3. Work the queue — triage alerts by severity, oldest-critical first.
4. Document everything in the case/ticket system — if it isn't written down, it didn't happen.
5. Stand-up / sync — brief T2/lead on anything escalated.
6. End-of-shift handover — write the pass-down for the next analyst.

THE GOLDEN RULE
Consistency and documentation beat heroics. Every action is repeatable, auditable, and explainable to the next analyst, an auditor, or a court.
Tier-1 Triage — Working an Alert
Is it real? The core T1 decision
TIER 1
The job: an alert fires. The analyst must quickly and consistently decide: real threat or false positive? Then close it or escalate it — with notes.

TRIAGE STEPS
1. Read the alert — what rule fired, on what asset, for which user?
2. Enrich — reputation of IPs/domains/hashes (VirusTotal, TIP), asset criticality, user role, recent behaviour.
3. Validate — true positive, false positive, or benign-true-positive (real but authorised)?
4. Severity — impact × confidence → Sev 1-4.
5. Act — close-as-FP (and note why, for tuning), or escalate to T2 with your findings.
TRIAGE NOTE TEMPLATE
Alert      : <rule name / ID>
Asset/User : <host> / <account>
Observed   : <what actually happened>
Enrichment : VT <score>, asset crit <H/M/L>, user <role>
Verdict    : TP / FP / benign-TP
Severity   : Sev<1-4>
Action     : escalated to T2  /  closed (reason)
The SOC Toolset
What sits on the analyst's screens
TOOLS
How the tools fit together: logs flow into the SIEM, endpoints are watched by EDR, repetitive steps run in SOAR, intel lives in the TIP, and every alert becomes a case in the ticketing system.

THE STACK
SIEM (Splunk, Microsoft Sentinel, QRadar, Elastic) — aggregates logs, correlates, raises alerts. The analyst's home base.
EDR / XDR (CrowdStrike, Defender, SentinelOne) — endpoint visibility + response: isolate a host, kill a process, pull a file.
SOAR (Cortex XSOAR, Tines, Splunk SOAR) — automates playbooks: auto-enrich, auto-contain, reduce manual toil.
TIP (MISP, Anomali, OpenCTI) — stores and shares threat intel / IOCs.
Case management (TheHive, ServiceNow, Jira) — tickets, timelines, evidence, SLAs.
Malware sandbox (Any.run, Joe Sandbox, Cuckoo) — detonate suspicious files/URLs safely.
Tier-2 Investigation
Scoping and root cause
TIER 2
The job: T2 takes an escalated alert and answers — how bad is it, how far did it spread, and how did it start?

THE 5 Ws
What happened · When (build a timeline) · Where (which hosts/accounts/data) · Who (which user/identity) · How (entry vector + techniques used).

INVESTIGATION MOVES
Pivot across data sources (one IOC → all related events), build a timeline (super-timeline of the intrusion), scope the blast radius, and find root cause (patient zero + initial access). Map observed activity to MITRE ATT&CK to understand intent and predict next steps.

OUTPUT
A clear picture that drives the containment decision and feeds the incident report. If it is a confirmed incident, IR kicks in.
Incident Response Lifecycle
SANS PICERL / NIST 800-61 in practice
IR
The framework: SANS PICERL (= NIST SP 800-61 phases). Real IR is rarely linear — you loop between containment, eradication and analysis as you learn more.

THE SIX PHASES
1. Preparation — tooling, runbooks, contacts, training, backups before anything happens.
2. Identification — confirm a real incident, classify severity, declare it.
3. Containment — short-term (isolate the host, disable the account) then long-term (block C2, reset creds).
4. Eradication — remove the foothold: malware, persistence, attacker accounts.
5. Recovery — restore systems from known-good, monitor closely for re-infection.
6. Lessons Learned — blameless post-incident review; fix the gaps and tune detections.

KEY PRINCIPLE
Preserve evidence before you wipe: image and hash systems, maintain chain of custody — you may need it for legal, insurance, or regulators.
Runbooks & Playbooks
Consistency under pressure
PLAYBOOKS
Runbook = step-by-step for a single task (e.g. "isolate a host in EDR"). Playbook = the end-to-end workflow for an incident type. They make response consistent regardless of who is on shift.

PHISHING PLAYBOOK (example)
1. Confirm report (user-reported / detected)
2. Pull the email + headers; extract URLs, attachments, sender
3. Detonate URL/attachment in sandbox; check VT/TIP
4. Search mail logs: who else received it? who clicked?
5. Contain: quarantine across mailboxes, block sender/URL
6. If creds entered -> force password reset + revoke sessions + check sign-in logs
7. Document, add IOCs to TIP, brief users
RANSOMWARE PLAYBOOK (high level)
1. Isolate affected hosts immediately (EDR network-contain)
2. Identify variant + scope of encryption
3. Preserve evidence; do NOT power off (lose memory)
4. Disable spread: accounts, shares, lateral paths
5. Engage IR lead, management, legal; check backups
6. Eradicate + restore from known-good backups
7. Post-incident review + regulatory notification if required
Escalation, Severity & Comms
Knowing when to pull the alarm
ESCALATION
When to escalate: confirmed compromise, critical asset involved, active attacker, data at risk, or anything beyond your tier's authority. When in doubt, escalate — under-reacting costs more than over-reacting.

SEVERITY MATRIX (typical)
Sev 1 — Critical: active breach / business-critical impact → wake people up, war room.
Sev 2 — High: confirmed incident, contained scope → IR team, hourly updates.
Sev 3 — Medium: suspicious, needs investigation → normal workflow.
Sev 4 — Low: minor / policy → log and monitor.

COMMUNICATIONS
A major incident pulls in more than the SOC: IR lead, management/execs, Legal, PR/Comms, HR, and sometimes customers. Run a war room / bridge call, assign a scribe, and give regular status updates. Remember regulatory clocks — e.g. GDPR breach notification within 72 hours.
Metrics, KPIs & Alert Fatigue
How a SOC measures itself
METRICS
Why metrics matter: they prove value, expose bottlenecks, and protect analysts from burnout.

THE KEY METRICS
MTTD — Mean Time to Detect · MTTA — Mean Time to Acknowledge · MTTR — Mean Time to Respond/Resolve · Dwell time — how long the attacker was in undetected.
Plus: alert volume, false-positive rate, escalation rate, SLA adherence, and ATT&CK detection coverage.

ALERT FATIGUE — THE REAL ENEMY
Too many low-value alerts burn analysts out and cause real threats to be missed. Mature SOCs relentlessly tune noisy rules, automate enrichment with SOAR, and track FP rate as a first-class metric. A good detection is high-signal, low-noise, and mapped to a real TTP.
Threat Intelligence in the SOC
Intel-driven detection & response
CTI
How CTI is used: threat intelligence turns "an alert fired" into "this matches a known actor targeting our sector — here is what they do next."

THREE LEVELS
Tactical — IOCs (hashes, IPs, domains) feeding detections. Operational — TTPs & campaigns (ATT&CK). Strategic — who targets us and why (for leadership).

IN PRACTICE
Feeds + a TIP (MISP/OpenCTI) supply IOCs that auto-enrich alerts; analysts map activity to ATT&CK to anticipate the attacker; the SOC tracks relevant threat actors and converts intel into new detections. Remember the Pyramid of Pain — chasing IOCs is necessary but detecting TTPs is what really disrupts adversaries.

The Blue Team tab covers the detection rules; this is about the intel that prioritises and guides them.
Roles, Skills & Career Path
Breaking in and moving up
CAREERS
The ladder: SOC Analyst T1 → T2 → T3 / Threat Hunter → then specialise: Detection Engineer, Incident Response Lead, CTI Analyst, or SOC Manager.

CORE SKILLS
Log analysis, networking fundamentals (TCP/IP, DNS, HTTP), Windows & Linux internals, basic scripting (Python/PowerShell), MITRE ATT&CK fluency — and crucially, staying calm and methodical under pressure.

CERTIFICATIONS THAT MAP TO THE ROLE
Entry: CompTIA Security+, CySA+, Blue Team Level 1 (BTL1), Microsoft SC-200.
Mid/Senior: GIAC GCIH (incident handling), GCIA (intrusion analyst), GCFA/GCFE (forensics), GNFA (network forensics).
Hands-on practice: TryHackMe SOC path, LetsDefend, Blue Team Labs Online, CyberDefenders.

This dashboard's Skill Tree, Mini CTF and Blue Team tabs are good practice grounds.
Exercises & Continuous Improvement
Getting better between incidents
IMPROVE
A SOC improves on purpose, not by accident. The quiet time between incidents is where maturity is built.

KEY PRACTICES
Tabletop exercises — walk through a scenario ("ransomware on a file server") with all stakeholders; find gaps in the plan before a real incident does.
Purple teaming — red emulates a technique (Atomic Red Team / CALDERA), blue confirms it was detected; close any gap with a new rule.
Blameless post-incident reviews — focus on process and system fixes, not blame, so people report honestly.
Detection tuning loop — every false positive and every missed detection feeds back into the rules and runbooks.

THE OUTCOME
Lower MTTD/MTTR over time, less alert fatigue, broader ATT&CK coverage, and a team that has already rehearsed the bad day before it arrives.
Interactive Triage Simulator
Work a live alert queue — make the call, then see the analyst's verdict
Click START to begin.
Detection Performance Scorecard
MTTR & accuracy derived from your real lab activity
Loading…
Metrics come from your solved Challenges + AI-Lab Sandbox levels (set a username in Challenges or log in via AI Lab). Triage Accuracy comes from the simulator.
Playbook Library
Built-in runbooks + your own — click a card to expand
+ ADD YOUR OWN PLAYBOOK
