☠
GHOST COMMAND
TRAINING RANGE — LIVE FIRE
THREATCON BRAVO
SESSION 00:00:00
GHOST ONLINE
👻
GHOST // red-cell operator — keep it legal, keep it lethal
◆ HACKING PRACTICE RANGE — LEGAL HANDS-ON LABS
// START HERE
◆ The Golden Rule
Learning Path
// PLATFORMS
HackTheBox
TryHackMe
PortSwigger Web Security Academy
pwn.college & picoCTF
OverTheWire & VulnHub
// DISCIPLINE
Authorisation, Scope & the Law
Legal Practice Range
Only hack what you own or are explicitly authorised to test
RULE 0
The one rule that matters: only ever attack systems you own or have written permission to test. Unauthorised access is a crime (US CFAA, UK Computer Misuse Act, etc.) regardless of intent. Everything below is a legal sandbox built for exactly this — real, vulnerable machines you are invited to break.

WHY RANGES BEAT 'real targets'
They are intentionally vulnerable, reset-able, legal, and graded — you learn faster with hints, write-ups, and a difficulty ladder. This is how OSCP holders and pros actually train.
Beginner to advanced, in order
A SUGGESTED ROUTE
ROADMAP
0-3 MONTHS — FOUNDATIONS
TryHackMe "Complete Beginner" + "Jr Penetration Tester" paths · OverTheWire Bandit (Linux/CLI) · PortSwigger Academy (web basics).

3-9 MONTHS — REAL BOXES
HackTheBox Starting Point then easy retired machines (follow Ippsec videos) · PortSwigger Academy (all topics) · picoCTF for variety.

9+ MONTHS — EXAM/PRO
HTB Pro Labs / OSCP-like boxes (TJnull's list) · pwn.college for binary exploitation · then sit OSCP / PNPT.
The industry-standard hands-on range
FREE + PAID
HTB
HackTheBox (hackthebox.com) — hundreds of real vulnerable machines to root, plus structured learning.

WHERE TO START
Starting Point: guided tier-0 machines that teach the workflow. HTB Academy: modular courses (very good, OSCP-aligned). Retired machines: have official write-ups + Ippsec walkthroughs. Pro Labs: full simulated enterprise AD networks.

Best for: realistic end-to-end box-rooting, AD practice, exam prep.
Guided, beginner-friendly learning
FREE + PAID
THM
TryHackMe (tryhackme.com) — in-browser, guided "rooms" with step-by-step hand-holding. The gentlest on-ramp.

HIGHLIGHTS
Structured learning paths (Complete Beginner, Jr Pentester, SOC Level 1, Red Teaming). Browser-based attack box — no VPN/VM setup needed to start. Great for fundamentals and filling knowledge gaps.

Best for: total beginners, theory + practice combined, blue-team paths too.
The best free web-hacking trainer
100% FREE
WEB
PortSwigger Web Security Academy (portswigger.net/web-security) — free, world-class, made by the creators of Burp Suite.

WHY IT IS ELITE
Every major web vuln class with theory + interactive labs (real exploitable apps) graded automatically. Covers SQLi, XSS, SSRF, access control, JWT, deserialization, prototype pollution, GraphQL, and more — from apprentice to expert.

Best for: serious web/bug-bounty skills. Pair with the Web Hacking Deep-Dive tab here.
Binary exploitation & CTF
FREE
PWN/CTF
pwn.college (ASU) — a free, structured university-grade course in binary exploitation and low-level security, with auto-graded challenges.

picoCTF (Carnegie Mellon) — beginner-friendly CTF with a permanent practice gym across web, crypto, forensics, reversing and pwn.

ALSO
ROP Emporium (rop focused), Nightmare (CTF pwn course), crackmes.one (reversing). See the Exploit Dev tab.
Wargames + downloadable VMs
FREE
CLASSICS
OverTheWire (overthewire.org) — SSH wargames; start with Bandit (Linux + CLI mastery), then Natas (web), Leviathan, Krypton.

VulnHub (vulnhub.com) — free downloadable vulnerable VMs you run locally in VirtualBox/VMware — fully offline, fully legal, great for OSCP-style practice.

Also: Hack The Box "Sherlocks" (DFIR), CyberDefenders & LetsDefend (blue team), Root-Me, VulnLab.
Stay legal, stay employable
NON-NEGOTIABLE
LEGAL
Authorisation is everything. The difference between a pentester and a criminal is a signed scope document.

THE RULES
► Only test systems you own or have explicit written permission for.
► In bug bounty, read the program scope/policy before touching anything — out-of-scope testing can be illegal even on a bounty site.
► Never test production systems of others "to see if it works".
► Laws: US CFAA, UK Computer Misuse Act 1990, EU equivalents — unauthorised access alone is a crime.

Build a home lab (VirtualBox + Kali + VulnHub VMs) for anything you cannot do on a sanctioned range.
☠ OFFENSIVE OPERATIONS // RED CELL // AUTHORIZED TARGETS ONLY ☠
☠
GHOST COMMAND
PENETRATION OPERATIONS
THREATCON BRAVO
SESSION 00:00:00
GHOST ONLINE
👻
GHOST // red-cell operator — scope first, glory second
◆ PENETRATION TESTING METHODOLOGY — OSCP STYLE
// ENGAGEMENT
◆ The Engagement Lifecycle
// PHASES
Recon & Enumeration
Initial Foothold
Privilege Escalation
Post-Exploitation & Pivoting
// DELIVER
The Report
OSCP Exam Prep
Authorized Penetration Testing
How a real, sanctioned pentest runs end to end
METHOD
A pentest is authorised, scoped, and documented. It follows a repeatable lifecycle (PTES / OSCP model):

THE PHASES
1. Pre-engagement — scope, rules of engagement, signed authorisation.
2. Recon / Enumeration — map the attack surface (the phase that wins engagements).
3. Exploitation — gain an initial foothold.
4. Post-exploitation — privesc, looting, pivoting.
5. Reporting — the actual deliverable: findings, risk, remediation.

OSCP mantra: "Enumerate harder." 80% of the work is recon. (For detection of all this, see the Blue Team tab.)
Map everything before you touch anything
ENUMERATE HARDER
PHASE 1
On a lab/authorised target, enumerate exhaustively: ports, services, versions, web content, users.

TYPICAL FIRST MOVES
# full TCP port scan, then service/version on open ports
nmap -p- --min-rate 1000 TARGET
nmap -sC -sV -p 22,80,443 TARGET

# web content + vhost discovery
gobuster dir -u http://TARGET -w /usr/share/wordlists/dirb/common.txt
ffuf -u http://TARGET/FUZZ -w wordlist.txt
whatweb http://TARGET
Read everything: HTTP headers, robots.txt, source, error messages, SMB/NFS shares, SNMP. Every detail is a potential foothold.
Turning a finding into access
PHASE 2
PHASE 2
Common foothold routes on lab targets:

FIND A KNOWN EXPLOIT
# search local exploit DB for a service+version you enumerated
searchsploit apache 2.4.49
searchsploit -m 50383   # copy a PoC locally to study
OTHER VECTORS
► Web flaws (see Web Hacking tab: SQLi, file upload, LFI/RFI, command injection).
► Default/weak credentials, exposed admin panels.
► Password attacks on a service you are authorised to test:
hydra -L users.txt -P rockyou.txt ssh://TARGET   # lab target only
Always understand a PoC before running it — never fire code you cannot read.
From low-priv to admin/root
PHASE 3
PHASE 3
The first step is always enumeration — automate it, then verify by hand.

LINUX
./linpeas.sh            # automated enum
sudo -l                 # what can I run as root?
find / -perm -4000 2>/dev/null   # SUID binaries
Check GTFOBins for any SUID/sudo binary you find. Look at cron jobs, writable files, capabilities, kernel version.

WINDOWS
winPEAS.exe             # automated enum
whoami /priv            # token privileges
Check service permissions, unquoted paths, AlwaysInstallElevated, stored creds. LOLBAS is the Windows equivalent of GTFOBins.
Consolidate and move
PHASE 4
PHASE 4
Once you have access (on the authorised network): loot, establish a stable shell, and reach further systems.

TYPICAL ACTIVITIES
► Upgrade to a fully interactive TTY; establish reliable access for the test window.
► Loot: config files, credentials, keys, database dumps (document everything for the report).
► Pivot: use the foothold to reach segmented hosts (SSH tunnels, chisel, proxychains).
► In AD labs: practise the techniques in the Gov Hack / Blue Team AD panels (Kerberoasting, BloodHound paths).

Everything you do is logged for the client and is reversible/cleaned up at the end.
The part that actually matters
WHERE THE VALUE IS
DELIVERABLE
The report is the product — a client pays for findings and fixes, not for you getting root.

A GOOD FINDING CONTAINS
► Title + severity (CVSS) · affected assets.
► Clear description + business impact.
► Reproduction steps + evidence (screenshots).
► Concrete remediation guidance.

Structure: executive summary (for leadership) + technical detail (for engineers). Clear writing is a genuine, underrated pentest skill.
Try Harder
CERTIFICATION
OSCP
OSCP (OffSec Certified Professional) — a 24-hour hands-on exam: compromise machines in a lab network, then write a professional report.

HOW TO PREP
► Practice boxes: TJnull's OSCP-like list on HTB + PG Practice.
► Master a repeatable enumeration checklist — most fails are missed enumeration.
► Time + stress management; take breaks; document as you go (you need the report).
► Alternatives/complements: PNPT (TCM), HTB CPTS.

Mindset: "Try Harder" means methodical persistence, not banging your head — when stuck, enumerate something you skipped.
☠ OFFENSIVE OPERATIONS // RED CELL // AUTHORIZED TARGETS ONLY ☠
☠
GHOST COMMAND
WEB EXPLOITATION CELL
THREATCON BRAVO
SESSION 00:00:00
GHOST ONLINE
👻
GHOST // red-cell operator — every input is an opportunity
◆ WEB HACKING DEEP-DIVE — OWASP & BEYOND
// FOUNDATION
◆ Web Hacking 101
// INJECTION
SQL Injection
Command & Template Injection
// CLIENT & ACCESS
Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)
Broken Access Control & IDOR
// ADVANCED
SSRF
Recon & Tooling
// HANDS-ON
▶ SQLi Lab (live)
▶ XSS Lab (live)
▶ Cmd Injection Lab
OWASP Top 10 & methodology
How web vulns work and how to find them — on apps you own/are authorised to test
WEB
🎯 HANDS-ON LABS:
Web is the #1 attack surface, and the best-documented to learn legally (PortSwigger Academy + your own DVWA/Juice Shop).

THE TESTING LOOP
Map the app (every page, parameter, endpoint) → discover (probe each input for anomalies) → exploit (prove impact) → report (with a fix).

OWASP TOP 10 (2021)
Broken Access Control · Cryptographic Failures · Injection · Insecure Design · Security Misconfiguration · Vulnerable Components · Auth Failures · Software/Data Integrity · Logging Failures · SSRF.

Each panel below: how it works, a lab-level example, and the defense (the part that makes you employable).
Injecting into database queries
INJECTION
SQLi
What: user input reaches a SQL query unsanitised, letting you alter its logic. The classic auth bypass:
username: admin'--
-- query becomes: SELECT * FROM users WHERE name='admin'--' AND pass='...'
TYPES
UNION-based (extract data via UNION SELECT), Blind boolean (true/false inference), Time-based (sleep delays). On lab targets, sqlmap -u URL --batch automates it.

DEFENSE
Parameterised queries / prepared statements — input is data, never code. Plus least-privilege DB accounts and input validation. (Detection: see Blue Team → Web Exploit.)
Server-side code execution
INJECTION
RCE
OS Command Injection: input concatenated into a shell command. Lab probe: ; id or | whoami in a vulnerable field.

SSTI (Server-Side Template Injection): input rendered by a template engine. Detect with a math probe:
input: {{7*7}}      -> renders 49  => template injection (Jinja2/Twig/etc.)
Can escalate to RCE depending on the engine.

DEFENSE
Never pass user input to a shell/template; use safe APIs, allow-lists, and sandboxed/logic-less templates.
Injecting JavaScript into pages
CLIENT-SIDE
XSS
What: your input is reflected into a page unescaped, so the browser runs it as script.

TYPES
Reflected (in the response to a request), Stored (saved + served to others — worst), DOM (client-side JS sink). Classic test payload:
<script>alert(document.domain)</script>
<img src=x onerror=alert(1)>
Impact: session/cookie theft, account takeover, keylogging.

DEFENSE
Context-aware output encoding, a strict Content-Security-Policy, HttpOnly cookies, framework auto-escaping.
Doing things you should not be allowed to
#1 OWASP RISK
ACCESS
IDOR: the app trusts a client-supplied ID without checking ownership.
GET /api/invoice?id=1001   ->   change to 1002 and read someone else's invoice
Other access flaws: forced browsing to admin URLs, missing function-level checks, privilege escalation via parameter tampering (role=admin).

DEFENSE
Enforce authorisation server-side on every request against the session identity — never trust client-supplied IDs or roles. Use unpredictable references + deny-by-default.
Make the server request for you
ADVANCED
SSRF
What: you control a URL the server fetches, so you reach internal systems the server can but you cannot.
url=http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/   # cloud metadata (AWS)
url=http://localhost:8080/admin                # internal-only service
A top cloud-era vuln — can lead to credential theft from metadata endpoints.

DEFENSE
Allow-list outbound destinations, block link-local/internal ranges, disable unused URL schemes, require authentication on internal services (IMDSv2 on AWS).
The web tester workflow
TRADECRAFT
TOOLS
Burp Suite is the core tool: intercepting proxy → map the app, then Repeater (manual tampering), Intruder (fuzzing), Decoder. The free Community edition is enough to learn.

RECON / DISCOVERY
ffuf -u https://TARGET/FUZZ -w content.txt          # content discovery
ffuf -u https://TARGET/ -H "Host: FUZZ.target" -w subs.txt  # vhosts
nuclei -u https://TARGET                              # templated checks
METHODOLOGY
Map every endpoint/parameter, test each input class against each vuln type, chain findings (e.g. SSRF → metadata → cloud takeover). Practice it all on PortSwigger Academy + OWASP Juice Shop.
Guided SQL Injection Lab
A simulated vulnerable login — practise real SQLi safely. The "database" is fake and runs in your browser; nothing real is attacked.
🔒 ACME Corp — Member Login
LIVE SQL QUERY — what the server builds from your input
 
Defense reminder: every bug here is killed by parameterised queries — the input is sent as data, never concatenated into SQL. See the SQL Injection panel.
Guided XSS Lab
Simulated reflected XSS — see exactly what the browser WOULD do with your payload. For safety nothing actually executes; the result is computed, not run.
🔍 Search our site
REFLECTED PAGE HTML — your input is dropped in unescaped
 
Defense: context-aware output encoding + a strict Content-Security-Policy + HttpOnly cookies. See the XSS panel.
Guided Command Injection Lab
A simulated "ping" tool that builds a shell command from your input. Fully simulated — no real shell runs — so you can practise injecting extra OS commands safely.
📡 Network Ping Tool
COMMAND THE SERVER RUNS — your input is concatenated into a shell string
 
Defense: never pass input to a shell — use exec-array APIs (no shell), strict input allow-lists, and least privilege. See the Command & Template Injection panel.

☠
GHOST COMMAND
BINARY EXPLOITATION CELL
THREATCON BRAVO
SESSION 00:00:00
 GHOST ONLINE
👻
GHOST // red-cell operator — mind the canary
◆ EXPLOIT DEV & CTF PWN — BINARY EXPLOITATION
// FOUNDATION
◆ Binary Exploitation 101
// FUNDAMENTALS
Memory & the Stack
Stack Buffer Overflow
Modern Mitigations
// TECHNIQUES
Return-Oriented Programming
Format Strings & Heap
// PRACTICE
Where to Practice
// HANDS-ON
▶ ret2win Lab (live)
CTF 'pwn' & exploit dev
Low-level memory-corruption, learned on CTF challenges + binaries you control
PWN
🎯 HANDS-ON LAB: 
What it is: making a compiled program do something it was not meant to by abusing memory-safety bugs. The CTF "pwn" category and the basis of real exploit development.

THE TOOLCHAIN
gdb + pwndbg/GEF (debugger), pwntools (Python exploit framework), Ghidra/IDA (disassembler/decompiler), checksec (see mitigations), ROPgadget.

LEARN IT ON
pwn.college (structured), ROP Emporium (rop), picoCTF, Nightmare. Everything here is for CTF challenges and lab binaries you own.
How function calls work
CONCEPT
STACK
Process memory is laid out as: text (code) · data/bss · heap (grows up) · stack (grows down).

THE STACK FRAME
On a function call the stack holds local buffers, the saved base pointer, and the saved return address (where execution resumes when the function returns). Control that return address and you control execution.

x86-64 args go in registers (rdi, rsi, rdx, ...); the return address sits on the stack. Understanding this is 90% of basic pwn.
The classic bug
CONCEPT + CTF
BOF
What: writing more into a stack buffer than it holds overwrites adjacent memory — including the saved return address.

THE WORKFLOW (on a CTF binary)
1. Find the offset to the return address (pwntools cyclic pattern).
2. Overwrite it with an address you choose (e.g. a "win" function in the binary).
from pwn import *
io = process('./vuln')
offset = 40                      # found via cyclic
payload = b'A'*offset + p64(win_addr)
io.sendline(payload)
io.interactive()
This is the "ret2win" pattern — the first real pwn challenge everyone solves.
Why it is harder now
DEFENSE
MITIGATIONS
checksec shows what protects a binary. CTFs are designed around bypassing these conceptually:

► NX / DEP: stack not executable → cannot just run shellcode on it → use ROP instead.
► Stack canary: a secret value before the return address; must be leaked, not overwritten blindly.
► ASLR / PIE: addresses randomised → need an info-leak to defeat.
► RELRO: protects the GOT from overwrites.

Defender takeaway: compile with all of these on (-fstack-protector-all, PIE, full RELRO) and prefer memory-safe languages.
Code-reuse when the stack is NX
ADVANCED
ROP
Why: NX stops your own shellcode, so you reuse tiny existing instruction sequences ("gadgets") ending in ret to build behaviour from the program's own code.

THE IDEA
Chain gadget addresses on the stack so each ret jumps to the next. A common goal is ret2libc — call system("/bin/sh") using libc. ROPgadget --binary ./vuln finds gadgets; pwntools ROP() auto-builds chains.

Best practiced step-by-step at ROP Emporium, which is built exactly for this.
Beyond the stack
ADVANCED
MORE BUGS
Format-string bugs: passing user input as the format argument (printf(user)) lets you leak memory with %p or write with %n — often used to defeat ASLR/canaries via leaks.

Heap exploitation: abusing the dynamic allocator (malloc/free) — use-after-free, double-free, tcache poisoning. This is the deep end; learn it after stack mastery.

DEFENSE
Never pass user input as a format string (compilers warn: -Wformat-security); modern allocators add hardening; memory-safe languages remove the bug class.
Build the skill for real
HANDS-ON
DO IT
STRUCTURED
► pwn.college — university-grade, auto-graded, start-to-advanced.
► ROP Emporium — focused ROP challenges in increasing difficulty.
► Nightmare — a full CTF pwn/reversing course (free, GitBook).

CHALLENGES
► picoCTF Binary Exploitation track · pwnable.kr / pwnable.tw · live CTFs via CTFtime (see the CTF Cal tab).

STARTER PWNTOOLS TEMPLATE
from pwn import *
elf = context.binary = ELF('./chal')
io = process()        # or remote('host', port) for a CTF
# ... build payload, leak, exploit ...
io.interactive()
Everything here is for CTF infrastructure and binaries you are given/own — the legal, intended playground.
Guided ret2win Lab
Simulated binary exploitation — find the offset to the saved return address, then redirect execution to win(). Nothing real runs; the crash and exploit are computed in your browser.
THE TARGET (vuln)
► vuln() reads input with gets(buf) into char buf[64] — no bounds check (overflow!).
► Stack layout: buf[64] + saved RBP (8) + saved return address (8).
► A win() function that prints the flag sits at 0x401256 — but is never called normally.
► Mitigations: checksec says No canary, NX enabled, No PIE (so win()'s address is fixed).
STAGE 1 — FIND THE OFFSET
 
Click "Generate cyclic pattern" to begin.
STAGE 2 — BUILD THE ret2win PAYLOAD
paddingreturn address
payload = b'A'*? + p64(?)
 
This is the "ret2win" pattern every pwn learner solves first. Practise the real thing at ROP Emporium & pwn.college.


◆ TOP SECRET // DEFENSIVE OPERATIONS COMMAND // NOFORN ◆
🛡
USCYBERCOM · DEFENSIVE OPERATIONS
DIGITAL FORENSICS WING
THREATCON BRAVO
SHIFT 00:00:00
 SENTINEL ONLINE
🤖
SENTINEL // defensive intelligence — at your service, Operator
◆ DFIR — DIGITAL FORENSICS & INCIDENT RESPONSE
// FUNDAMENTALS
◆ DFIR Overview
The IR Process
Evidence Acquisition
// FORENSIC DOMAINS
Disk & File-System Forensics
Memory Forensics
Windows Investigation
Linux Investigation
Network Forensics
Document & Email Forensics
// ANALYSIS
Malware Analysis & IOCs
Timeline Analysis
SIEM & Threat Hunting
// REFERENCE
DFIR Toolkit
Certs, Practice & Careers
// HANDS-ON
▶ Investigation Lab
Digital Forensics & Incident Response
Find, preserve, analyse evidence — then contain, eradicate, recover
DFIR
🎯 HANDS-ON LAB: 
DFIR = Digital Forensics (scientifically recover, preserve and analyse digital evidence) + Incident Response (detect, contain, eradicate and recover from an intrusion). Forensics tells you what happened; IR acts on it.

CORE FORENSIC PRINCIPLES
► Order of volatility: collect the most fragile evidence first (RAM → network state → disk → backups).
► Forensic soundness: never work on the original — image it, hash it, work on the copy.
► Integrity: hash evidence before and after (MD5/SHA-256) to prove it was not altered.
► Chain of custody: document who handled what, when — for legal admissibility.

COURSE OBJECTIVES (modeled on a 120-hr DFIR program for SOC analysts)
IR frameworks · document/email forensics · malware identification · network anomalies · Windows & Linux investigation · timeline analysis · IOC/TTP development · SIEM big-data · threat hunting. Each is a panel here.
NIST 800-61 / SANS PICERL in DFIR
FRAMEWORK
PROCESS
Frameworks: SANS PICERL = NIST SP 800-61 phases. DFIR work lives mainly in Detection & Analysis and Containment/Eradication/Recovery.

THE SIX PHASES
1. Preparation — tooling, jump kit, runbooks, baselines.
2. Identification — confirm + scope the incident from forensic evidence.
3. Containment — isolate, but preserve volatile evidence first (image RAM before pulling the plug).
4. Eradication — remove malware, persistence, attacker access.
5. Recovery — restore from known-good, monitor.
6. Lessons Learned — report + improve detections.

See the SOC / IR tab for how a team operationalises this.
Imaging, hashing, chain of custody
ACQUISITION
EVIDENCE
Golden rule: acquire a bit-for-bit copy, hash it, and only ever analyse the copy.

DISK IMAGING
Use a write blocker (hardware or software) so you cannot alter the source. Tools: FTK Imager, Guymager, dd/dcfldd, EWF (.E01).
# image a disk and prove integrity
dd if=/dev/sdb of=evidence.img bs=4M conv=noerror,sync status=progress
sha256sum evidence.img > evidence.img.sha256   # hash BEFORE and AFTER
ORDER OF VOLATILITY
CPU registers/cache → RAM → network connections/ARP → running processes → disk → logs on remote hosts → archival media. Capture memory before you shut down — most malware lives there.

Chain of custody: every transfer logged (who/what/when/why) so the evidence holds up legally.
Recovering the story from storage
DOMAIN
DISK
File systems: NTFS is the big one — its metadata is a goldmine.

KEY NTFS ARTIFACTS
► $MFT (Master File Table) — every file's metadata + timestamps.
► $LogFile / $UsnJrnl — file-system change journals (creations, deletions, renames).
► Prefetch (execution evidence), ShimCache/AmCache (programs that ran), LNK & Jump Lists (file access), Recycle Bin.

TOOLS & CARVING
Autopsy / The Sleuth Kit, X-Ways, FTK, KAPE (fast triage collection), Eric Zimmerman tools (MFTECmd, PECmd). Recover deleted files by carving (PhotoRec, scalpel).
mmls evidence.img                 # partition layout
fls -r -m / evidence.img          # list files (incl. deleted) as timeline
icat evidence.img 12345 > out     # extract a file by inode
What only RAM can tell you
DOMAIN
MEMORY
Why RAM matters: injected code, decrypted payloads, credentials, network connections and fileless malware exist only in memory.

CAPTURE
WinPMEM, DumpIt, Magnet RAM Capture (Windows); LiME, AVML (Linux). Capture before containment.

ANALYSIS — VOLATILITY
vol.py -f mem.raw windows.pslist        # processes
vol.py -f mem.raw windows.malfind       # injected / hidden code
vol.py -f mem.raw windows.netscan       # network connections
vol.py -f mem.raw windows.cmdline       # process command lines
vol.py -f mem.raw windows.dlllist       # loaded DLLs
Hunt for: hollowed/injected processes, unlinked processes, suspicious network sockets, and known-bad in-memory strings (YARA). Volatility 3 + MemProcFS are the standard.
Registry, event logs, artifacts
DOMAIN
WINDOWS
REGISTRY
Hives: SYSTEM, SOFTWARE, SAM, SECURITY, NTUSER.DAT. Look at Run keys, Services, USB history, RecentDocs, UserAssist. Tool: RegRipper.

EVENT LOGS (key IDs)
4624/4625 logon/fail · 4672 admin logon · 4688 process create · 4698 scheduled task · 7045 service install · 1102 log cleared · Sysmon 1/3/11/13. Tool: EvtxECmd, Chainsaw, Hayabusa.

EXECUTION & ACTIVITY ARTIFACTS
Prefetch (PECmd), ShimCache/AmCache (AppCompatCacheParser), SRUM (resource usage), $MFT/$UsnJrnl (file activity), LNK + Jump Lists (file access), browser history. The Eric Zimmerman toolset parses all of these.
Logs, history, persistence
DOMAIN
LINUX
LOGS
/var/log/: auth.log/secure (logins, sudo), syslog, journald (journalctl), web/app logs. Watch for new users, sudo abuse, SSH brute force.

USER & EXECUTION TRACES
~/.bash_history, ~/.ssh/ (authorized_keys!), /etc/passwd & shadow, last/lastb, file timestamps (stat, debugfs for ext4).

PERSISTENCE TO CHECK
cron (/etc/cron*, crontabs), systemd units & timers, rc.local, shell rc files, LD_PRELOAD, SUID binaries, kernel modules.
last -f /var/log/wtmp            # login history
find / -newermt "2026-06-19" -type f 2>/dev/null   # recently modified
cat ~/.bash_history; crontab -l
PCAP & flow analysis
DOMAIN
NETWORK
Goal: spot anomalies in traffic — C2 beaconing, data exfiltration, lateral movement, tunnelling.

TOOLS
Wireshark / tshark / tcpdump (packets), Zeek (conn/dns/http/ssl logs), Suricata (IDS alerts), NetworkMiner (artifact extraction), NetFlow.
tshark -r cap.pcap -Y "dns" -T fields -e dns.qry.name | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn
tshark -r cap.pcap -q -z conv,tcp        # top talkers / sessions
WHAT TO LOOK FOR
► Beaconing — regular interval, low jitter to one host.
► DNS tunnelling — long, high-entropy subdomains.
► Large outbound transfers (exfil), odd ports, JA3/JARM TLS fingerprints of known C2.
Malicious docs, PDFs, RTF, email
DELIVERY
DOCS
The #1 delivery vector — and a core objective of this course. Analyse suspicious attachments in an isolated VM.

OFFICE DOCUMENTS
VBA macros, embedded objects, DDE, metadata. Tools: oletools (olevba, oleid, oledump).
olevba suspicious.docm        # extract + flag VBA macros
oleid suspicious.doc          # indicators overview
PDF & RTF
PDFs: embedded JavaScript, /OpenAction, embedded files (pdfid, pdf-parser). RTF: exploit objects (rtfobj).

EMAIL
Analyse headers (Received chain, true origin, SPF/DKIM/DMARC), sender spoofing, URLs and attachments. Reconstruct the phishing chain; extract IOCs.
Triage, analyse, extract intel
DEFENSIVE
MALWARE
Framed for the responder: analyse a sample to understand, detect, and respond — extract IOCs/TTPs, not build malware.
STATIC (don't run it)
Hashes, strings/FLOSS, PE headers + imports (PEStudio), capabilities (CAPA), YARA matches, reversing (Ghidra/IDA).

DYNAMIC (detonate safely)
Run in an isolated sandbox (Any.run, CAPE, Cuckoo, Joe) and watch process, file, registry and network behaviour.

PRODUCE INTEL
Extract IOCs (hashes, C2 domains/IPs, mutexes, dropped files, registry keys) and map behaviour to MITRE ATT&CK TTPs, then turn them into detections (see Blue Team).
Reconstruct the attack, minute by minute
CORE SKILL
TIMELINE
Timeline analysis stitches artifacts from every source into one chronological story — the heart of an investigation.

SUPER-TIMELINE
Combine $MFT, event logs, registry, browser, prefetch, etc. into a single timeline with plaso / log2timeline, then explore in Timesketch.
log2timeline.py --storage-file ts.plaso evidence.img
psort.py -o l2tcsv -w timeline.csv ts.plaso
MAC(b) TIMES & ANTI-FORENSICS
Modified / Accessed / Changed / Birth times tell the order of events. Watch for timestomping — compare $STANDARD_INFO vs $FILE_NAME timestamps in the $MFT; a mismatch is a red flag.
Big-data + hypothesis-driven hunting
SCALE
HUNT
SIEM big-data (Splunk, Elastic, Sentinel) lets you search across the whole estate — a course objective.

THREAT HUNTING
Form a hypothesis from an ATT&CK technique → query telemetry → confirm or clear → turn findings into a detection. Sweep IOCs fleet-wide.

HUNTING & IR AT SCALE
Velociraptor / GRR — run forensic queries (VQL) across thousands of endpoints at once. Chainsaw / Hayabusa — blazing-fast EVTX hunting with Sigma rules.
# Hayabusa: hunt EVTX logs with built-in Sigma rules
hayabusa csv-timeline -d ./EventLogs -o results.csv
The standard kit by category
REFERENCE
TOOLS
ACQUISITION
FTK Imager · Guymager · dd/dcfldd · WinPMEM/DumpIt · LiME/AVML · KAPE (triage)

DISK / ARTIFACTS
Autopsy + The Sleuth Kit · X-Ways · Eric Zimmerman tools (MFTECmd, PECmd, EvtxECmd) · RegRipper · PhotoRec

MEMORY
Volatility 3 · MemProcFS · bulk_extractor

NETWORK
Wireshark / tshark · Zeek · Suricata · NetworkMiner

MALWARE / DOCS
YARA · CAPA · FLOSS · PEStudio · oletools · pdf-parser · Ghidra

TIMELINE / SCALE
plaso (log2timeline) · Timesketch · Velociraptor · GRR · Chainsaw · Hayabusa

Most are free/open-source — build a DFIR VM (SIFT Workstation) and practise.
Becoming a DFIR analyst
CAREER
CAREER
Based on a 120-hour DFIR program aimed at technically skilled SOC analysts moving into incident-response roles (prereqs: security experience + Windows/Linux/networking).

CERTIFICATIONS
GIAC GCFA (forensic analyst), GCFE (Windows forensics), GNFA (network forensics), GREM (malware reversing), GCIH (incident handling); EnCE; Blue Team Level 2 (BTL2).

HANDS-ON PRACTICE
HackTheBox Sherlocks (DFIR scenarios) · CyberDefenders (blue-team labs with real artifacts) · Ali Hadi's challenges · DFIR memory/disk image challenges · the SIFT Workstation.

Pairs directly with this dashboard's Blue Team (detections) and SOC / IR (operations) tabs.
DFIR Investigation Lab
Work a simulated compromise — examine the forensic artifacts and identify the malicious process, the C2 channel, and the persistence mechanism, then reconstruct the attack. All evidence is fictional.
Click START INVESTIGATION.


☠ OFFENSIVE OPERATIONS // RED CELL // AUTHORIZED TARGETS ONLY ☠
☠
GHOST COMMAND
ETHICAL HACKING — FULL KILL CHAIN
THREATCON BRAVO
SESSION 00:00:00
 GHOST ONLINE
👻
GHOST // red-cell operator — recon to root, with a permission slip
◆ ETHICAL HACKING — FULL KILL CHAIN (AUTHORIZED)
// FOUNDATIONS
◆ Ethical Hacking 101
Attack Methodology
Risk Assessment from Evidence
// RECON & ACCESS
OSINT on Organizations
Scanning & Enumeration
Credential Attacks
Gaining & Keeping Access
// TRADECRAFT
Authorized-Engagement OPSEC
Web Application Pentest
Evasion — AV / IDS / Firewall
Malware & Exploit Dev with Python
The Defender Perspective
// CERTIFY
Certs, Practice & Careers
// HANDS-ON
▶ Enumeration Range
Attack with permission, defend with insight
Modeled on an 80-hr Ethical Hacking program (SOC/IT/dev → pentest/red team)
CEH
🎯 HANDS-ON LAB: 
Ethical hacking is using the exact craft of the attacker — but with written authorisation — to find flaws before criminals do. The difference between a professional and a felon is a signed scope document and intent.

THE 5 PHASES
1. Reconnaissance · 2. Scanning & Enumeration · 3. Gaining Access · 4. Maintaining Access · 5. Covering Tracks — the classic methodology this course follows.

COURSE OBJECTIVES
Attack methodologies · risk assessment from technical evidence · org OSINT · enumeration + credential attacks · anonymous attack environments · web app pentest · the defender perspective · evasion (AV/IDS/firewall) · malware/exploit dev with Python. Each is a panel here.
► ~80 academic hours; entrance exam; prereqs: dev, networking, Linux, Windows
► Target: SOC analysts / IT / developers moving into offensive roles
► Golden rule: only ever test systems you own or are explicitly authorised to test
Repeatable process beats random poking
METHOD
METHOD
Frameworks that structure an engagement: PTES (pre-engagement → intel → threat modeling → vuln analysis → exploitation → post-ex → reporting), the Cyber Kill Chain, MITRE ATT&CK, and OWASP for web.

A real test is methodical and documented end-to-end — every action repeatable and tied to an objective. Deep dive in the Pentest / OSCP tab; map your findings to ATT&CK so the blue team can learn from them.
Turning a shell into a risk rating
ANALYSIS
RISK
Advanced risk assessment: a finding is only useful when tied to likelihood × impact and real business risk.

HOW TO RATE A FINDING
► Severity via CVSS (base/temporal/environmental).
► Exploitability + asset criticality + data exposure.
► Chained findings (low + low can equal critical).

Translate technical evidence into language leadership acts on: what could happen, how likely, and what to fix first. The report is the product (see Pentest tab).
Know the target before you touch it
RECON
OSINT
Open-source intelligence maps the attack surface passively — no packets to the target.

WHAT YOU GATHER
Employees + emails (LinkedIn, theHarvester), domains/subdomains (amass, crt.sh), exposed services (Shodan), tech stack, leaked credentials (breach data), Google dorks.
theHarvester -d target.com -b all
amass enum -passive -d target.com
# (use the OSINT tab + Practice Range to do this for real, on authorised targets)
Recon is 70% of the work — the better the map, the cleaner the attack.
Map services, then enumerate everything
RECON
SCAN
Turn the target list into a service map, then enumerate each service for a way in.

THE MOVES
nmap -p- --min-rate 1000 TARGET           # all ports
nmap -sC -sV -p <open> TARGET             # service/version + scripts
nmap --script smb-enum-shares,smb-os-discovery -p445 TARGET
Enumerate SMB/SNMP/LDAP/NFS/HTTP exhaustively; run vuln templates (nuclei). Every banner and misconfig is a potential foothold. (Try it in the Terminal tab.)
Passwords are still the front door
ACCESS
CREDS
On authorised targets, credentials are often the easiest way in.

TECHNIQUES
► Password spraying (one password, many users — avoids lockout).
► Brute force (hydra) against a service you may test.
► Hash cracking offline (hashcat / John).
► Default & reused credentials, credential stuffing.
hydra -L users.txt -P rockyou.txt ssh://TARGET     # lab/authorised only
hashcat -m 1000 ntlm_hashes.txt rockyou.txt        # offline cracking
DEFENSE
MFA everywhere, lockout/throttling, strong slow hashing (bcrypt/argon2), banned-password lists.
Foothold, then escalate
ACCESS
ACCESS
Getting in usually comes from a known service exploit, a web flaw, or stolen creds.

ROUTES
► Public service exploits (searchsploit / Metasploit, in authorised labs).
► Web vectors (see Web Hacking + the SQLi/XSS/Cmd labs).
► Then privilege escalation & lateral movement (see Pentest tab).

Maintaining access in a sanctioned test means a stable, documented foothold you clean up at the end — never silent, never permanent.
Discipline, not anonymity-for-crime
OPSEC
OPSEC
Framed for sanctioned red-team engagements and for understanding adversary tradecraft so defenders can spot it — not for evading law enforcement.
Why a clean attack environment matters: separate your operation from your identity, keep the test reproducible, and avoid collateral damage.

AUTHORIZED PRACTICE
A dedicated isolated attack VM (Kali) · redirectors / jump boxes so infrastructure is segmented · a VPN to a known egress · meticulous logging of your own actions for the report · deconfliction with the blue team. Understanding how real adversaries minimise their footprint is exactly what makes a SOC analyst better at catching them.
The #1 attack surface
ACCESS
WEB
Web app penetration follows a repeatable loop: map every endpoint/parameter → probe each input class → exploit → report with a fix.

Covers the OWASP Top 10: injection (SQLi), XSS, SSRF, broken access control/IDOR, SSTI, auth flaws. The core tool is Burp Suite.

Full breakdown + three live labs (SQLi, XSS, Command Injection) in the Web Hacking tab.
Why it works & how it is caught
DEFENDER-AWARE
EVASION
Intentionally conceptual and detection-focused. It explains the categories of evasion and how blue teams defeat them — it does not provide working AV/EDR bypass code.
Why evasion exists: signature-based defences match known-bad, so adversaries change the surface (not the behaviour).

CATEGORIES (concept)
► Payload obfuscation/encoding to dodge static signatures.
► Traffic shaping/encryption to blend C2 into normal flows.
► Timing (low-and-slow) to beat thresholds.
► Living-off-the-land — use built-in tools so nothing looks foreign.

HOW DEFENDERS WIN (ATT&CK T1027/T1562/T1071)
Behavioural EDR (sequences, not signatures), AMSI/ETW telemetry, egress filtering + TLS fingerprinting (JA3), and anomaly detection. See the Blue Team tab — evasion only beats lazy, signature-only defences.
PoC tooling, the educational way
PYTHON
PYTHON
Conceptual / proof-of-concept framing for authorised testing, CTF and learning. No turnkey malware — the goal is understanding, which also sharpens detection.
Python is the security Swiss-army knife. In an authorised engagement, custom proof-of-concept code proves a vulnerability is real.

LEGITIMATE PYTHON SECURITY USES
► scapy — craft/parse packets for network testing.
► sockets — simple client/server tools and listeners.
► pwntools — exploit PoCs for CTF challenges & lab binaries (see the ret2win lab).
► requests / BeautifulSoup — web testing automation.

Understanding how PoCs and implants are built is what lets defenders recognise and hunt them (see DFIR → Malware Analysis).
Know what you trip
BLUE
BLUE
A great attacker thinks like a defender. You must understand the systems you are testing to test them well and report meaningfully.

KNOW YOUR ADVERSARY-IN-REVERSE
SIEM (what gets logged), EDR (what behaviours alert), WAF/IDS/firewall (what is blocked), and forensics (what artifacts you leave). Every action you take is a potential detection — knowing which makes you both a better tester and a better blue-teamer.

This dashboard pairs it directly: Blue Team (detections), SOC / IR (operations), DFIR (forensics).
From course to red-team job
CAREER
CERT
Based on an ~80-hour ethical-hacking program (entrance exam; for SOC/IT/devs heading into pentest/red-team roles).

CERTIFICATIONS
Entry: eJPT, CompTIA PenTest+, CEH. Hands-on: OSCP (the gold standard), PNPT (TCM), HTB CPTS.

PRACTICE
HackTheBox, TryHackMe, PortSwigger Academy, VulnHub — all in the Practice Range tab. Build the methodology on real (legal) targets until it is muscle memory.
Enumeration Range — nmap Recon Puzzle
A simulated authorised box. Read the scan output, find the weak service, and chart the foothold. The target is fictional and fully simulated.
Click START SCAN.

🛰
SENTINEL COMMAND
CYBER INTELLIGENCE — FUSION CENTER
THREATCON BRAVO
SESSION 00:00:00
 SENTINEL ONLINE
🤖
SENTINEL // all-source fusion — see the pattern before they do
0
INTERCEPTS TODAY
0
ACTIVE OPERATIONS
ELEVATED
GLOBAL THREAT

ZULU TIME
